Unknown.narrative:

- CGIAR synthesized the state of knowledge on climate change impacts, adaptation, and mitigation (https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647). 
- CGIAR evaluated the environmental impacts of meeting future crop demand (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1116437108).
Unknown.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the ability of climate envelope models to predict species distributions under different climates (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2006.01256.x).
• CGIAR reviewed the risk of climate-related impacts along the livestock food supply chain (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2020.100488).
Unknown.mitigation:
• CGIAR revised and updated the planetary boundaries framework to guide human development (https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1259855).
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of land-use change and management on soil organic carbon (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-39338-z).
• CGIAR provided ecosystem-scale data on CO2 exchange through the FLUXNET2015 dataset (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0534-3).
Unknown.water: • CGIAR conducted a multimodel assessment of water scarcity under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222460110).

Kenya.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and agricultural adaptation in Kenya through innovative research and policy interventions. Specifically:
Kenya.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined farmers’ attitudes and determinants of adaptation to climate risks in Kenya, highlighting the importance of climate information for timely planting and promoting crop diversification (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001).
• CGIAR assessed the potential climate change impact on key rainfed cereals in Kenya and the potential of cultivar adaptation to mitigate these impacts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
• CGIAR explored the relationship between farming diversity and food security in Kenya, emphasizing the importance of agricultural diversification as a strategy for achieving food security (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
• CGIAR analyzed the prioritized climate-smart agricultural practices adopted among smallholder farmers in Kenya, focusing on good agricultural practices in the sweet potato value chain (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228).
Kenya.water:
• CGIAR evaluated the performance of ERA5 reanalysis in improving the representation of precipitation during extreme years in East Africa, including Kenya, which is crucial for agriculture and water resources (https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on hydrology and hydrological extremes in East Africa, including Kenya, highlighting the need for site-specific adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1029/2022ef003011).

Ethiopia.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation strategies in Ethiopia through various agricultural innovations. Specifically:
Ethiopia.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed farmers' perception and adaptation to climate change, highlighting factors like education, household size, and extension services (https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687).
• CGIAR investigated smallholder farmers’ adaptation strategies, including crop diversification and soil and water conservation, and identified key determinants like education and access to climate information (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-017-0100-1).
• CGIAR explored climate-smart agriculture practices, such as minimum tillage and cereal-legume intercropping, to reduce poverty and improve climate risk management (https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12161).
• CGIAR assessed farmers’ attitudes and determinants of adaptation to climate risks, emphasizing the importance of climate information and crop diversification (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.03.001).
• CGIAR evaluated climate risk management interventions, including stress-adapted crop germplasm and conservation agriculture, to stabilize production and reduce poverty (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR used citizen science to improve variety recommendations for climate adaptation, incorporating seasonal climate forecasts and risk analysis (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116).
• CGIAR projected shifts in Coffea arabica suitability due to climate change, highlighting decreased areas suitable for Arabica coffee (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155).
• CGIAR assessed agricultural emissions and identified mitigation and adaptation practices, such as crop rotations and reduced tillage (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597).
• CGIAR evaluated the potential of cultivar adaptation to climate change for key rainfed cereals, aiming to compensate for climate change impacts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
Ethiopia.mitigation: • CGIAR researched climate change mitigation and adaptation practices, including reduced tillage and application of manure in cereal crops and potatoes (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2022.2028597).
Ethiopia.water: • CGIAR evaluated the performance of ERA5 reanalysis for temperature and precipitation over East Africa, improving the representation of precipitation during extreme years (https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos11090996).

Vietnam.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and adaptation in Vietnam through innovative research and policy interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate suitability for coffee production and its impacts under changing climates (Source 1).
- CGIAR evaluated climate risk management strategies to reduce rural poverty (Source 2).
- CGIAR modeled shifts in climatic suitability for Arabica coffee due to climate change (Source 3).
- CGIAR developed a Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment to prioritize agricultural vulnerability (Source 4).
- CGIAR examined determinants of adoption of climate-smart agriculture technologies in rice production (Source 5).
- CGIAR reviewed gender-responsive rural climate services to enhance access to climate information (Source 6).
- CGIAR investigated barriers to implementing climate policies in agriculture (Source 7).
- CGIAR explored food and climate futures through participatory scenario processes (Source 9).
Vietnam.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate suitability for coffee production and projected reductions in suitable areas due to higher temperatures and increased variability of intra-seasonal temperatures (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1306-x).
• CGIAR evaluated climate risk management strategies to stabilize production and incomes, protect assets, and enhance uptake of improved technologies and practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR modeled shifts in climatic suitability for Arabica coffee due to climate change, predicting decreased areas suitable for production (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0124155).
• CGIAR developed a Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment to prioritize agricultural vulnerability, identifying hotspots and informing National Adaptation Plans (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641).
• CGIAR examined determinants of adoption of climate-smart agriculture technologies in rice production, highlighting factors affecting adoption decisions (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2019-0003).
• CGIAR reviewed gender-responsive rural climate services to enhance access to climate information and improve adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
• CGIAR investigated barriers to implementing climate policies in agriculture, evaluating bottlenecks and implications for NDC design (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2021.439881).

Colombia.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation, mitigation, and water management in Colombia through targeted research and policy interventions. Specifically:
Colombia.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed farmers’ and experts’ differing perceptions of climate-related and livelihood risks in Cauca, Colombia, highlighting the need for policies that relate climate change risks to other production risks (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2320-1).
• CGIAR developed the LCA4CSA framework to assess the environmental sustainability of climate-smart agriculture practices, such as using compost made from coffee processing residues, in southern Colombia (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001).
• CGIAR introduced data mining techniques to assess weather-yield relationships in rice, providing site-specific information on cultivar response to climatic factors and supporting on-farm management decisions for adaptation to climate variability in Colombia (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0161620).
• CGIAR presented a methodological framework to co-design climate-smart farming systems with local stakeholders, including farmers and scientists, to achieve large-scale change in Colombia (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates in Latin America, including Colombia, to understand the coupled impacts of climate change on crop suitability and pollination (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR analyzed the relationship between rural livelihood diversification and household-level vulnerability to climate change in the Andean-Amazon foothills of Colombia, concluding that households with higher rural livelihood diversification are less vulnerable to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051).
Colombia.mitigation:
• CGIAR investigated CO2 emission scenarios for Colombia and the effects of implementing carbon taxes and abatement targets on the energy system, promoting the entry of cleaner energy sources into the market (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eneco.2015.05.010).
• CGIAR explored the mitigation potentials of technologies and management practices to reduce cattle-based enteric methane emissions in Colombia, discussing barriers for adopting innovations and the major shifts in policy and practice needed to raise national ambitions (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00065).
• CGIAR assessed the mitigation potential of compost made from coffee processing residues and the emissions reduction potential by modifying the feeding of animals in family farming systems in southern Colombia (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001).
• CGIAR proposed elements for the design of Colombia's national program of livestock raising to facilitate large-scale adoption of mitigation and adaptation practices in the livestock sector (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00061).
• CGIAR presented an overview of recent, ongoing, and planned restoration initiatives in Latin America and the Caribbean, including Colombia, and their potential for climate change mitigation through forest landscape restoration and other restorative ecosystem management activities (https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510).
Colombia.water: • CGIAR introduced data mining techniques to assess weather-yield relationships in rice, providing site-specific information on cultivar response to climatic factors and supporting on-farm management decisions for adaptation to climate variability in Colombia (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0161620).

Senegal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies in Senegal through various innovations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture technologies and their challenges (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions for poverty reduction (Source 2).
Senegal.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies such as agroforestry, soil and water conservation, and climate information services for adaptation and risk management in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199).
• CGIAR assessed production technologies and institutional options for managing climate-related risk to stabilize production and incomes in risk-prone smallholder agricultural systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR explored the relationship between farming diversity and food security, highlighting the importance of agricultural diversification as an adaptation strategy (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
• CGIAR analyzed the nexus of climate-smart agriculture, food systems, and sustainable development to inform transformative agriculture under climate change pressure (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy developments for the livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR assessed gender-based differences in access, use, and benefits from rural climate services to identify pathways for making climate services more responsive to the needs of rural women and men (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
Senegal.water:
• CGIAR reviewed soil and water conservation technologies and climate information services as promising options for climate change adaptation and risk management in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199).
• CGIAR examined the approach used to involve stakeholders in the climate information services (CIS) uptake process in Senegal, highlighting the use of CIS for decision-making in farming, pastoral, and fishing management systems (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli6010013).

Peru.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation in Peru through diverse research initiatives. Specifically: 
- CGIAR explored genetic diversity for climate adaptation in plant breeding programs (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed crop diversity and resilience strategies among farmers (Source 2).
Peru.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the role of genetic resources in breeding for climate change adaptation, highlighting challenges in accessing germplasm (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
• CGIAR explored the role of crop diversity in farmers’ adaptation actions on the Bolivian Altiplano, identifying stress-tolerant crops (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-016-9906-4).
• CGIAR assessed the future impact of climate change on cacao in Peru and identified areas with climate change-tolerant genotypes (https://doi.org/10.1111/ddi.13294).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates, highlighting the need for management practices (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR studied the impact of climate shocks on agricultural practices among Peruvian farmers, focusing on soil conservation and water conservation measures (https://doi.org/10.1111/ajae.12177).
• CGIAR examined the relationship between rural livelihood diversification and household-level vulnerability to climate change in the Andean-Amazon foothills (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pclm.0000051).
Peru.mitigation:
• CGIAR reviewed land restoration initiatives in Latin America and the Caribbean, highlighting their potential for climate change mitigation (https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510).
• CGIAR assessed the alignment of REDD+ interventions with deforestation drivers in Peru, emphasizing the importance of addressing site-specific drivers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsci.2020.08.002).
• CGIAR conducted a comprehensive review of deforestation dynamics in Peru, highlighting the need for sustainable land use practices (https://doi.org/10.4160/cip.2023.12.007).

Mali.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in Mali through innovative monitoring and adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed near real-time agricultural monitoring systems for crop management (Source 1).
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on rainfed cereals and adaptation strategies (Source 3).
Mali.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the potential climate change impact on key rainfed cereals and the potential of cultivar adaptation in Mali (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
• CGIAR explored agricultural diversification as a strategy for achieving food security in Mali, highlighting the relationship between farming diversity and food security (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
• CGIAR analyzed the role of climate-smart agriculture in transforming food systems under climate change pressure in Mali (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy developments for the livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in Mali (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined smallholder farmer cropping decisions related to climate variability in Mali, focusing on adaptive capacity and farm-associated changes (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011).
• CGIAR investigated the relationship between climate-smart agriculture, land productivity, and food security in Mali, highlighting the benefits of bundling CSA practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697).
• CGIAR studied the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in Mali, using adapted improved maize varieties (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR assessed gender-based differences in access, use, and benefits from rural climate services in Mali, identifying pathways for making climate services more responsive to the needs of rural women and men (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
Mali.mitigation: • CGIAR examined the relationship between climate-smart agriculture, land productivity, and food security in Mali, highlighting the benefits of bundling CSA practices for reducing greenhouse gas emissions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697).
Mali.water:
• CGIAR developed the Sen2-Agri automated system for near real-time agriculture monitoring at the national scale in Mali, generating cloud-free composites, dynamic cropland masks, crop type maps, and vegetation status indicators (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007).
• CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture development and promotion in West Africa, focusing on soil and water conservation technologies and water conservation in Mali (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199).
• CGIAR studied the biophysical impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in Mali, examining the potential of adapted improved maize varieties under different nitrogen levels (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Philippines.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in the Philippines through innovative adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions for poverty reduction (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019). 
- CGIAR developed rice varieties adapted to changing climate conditions (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218).
Philippines.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers, focusing on stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR studied the effects of climate factors on rice quality, particularly chalkiness and head rice yield, to inform new breeding strategies for developing climate-adapted rice varieties (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218).
• CGIAR explored the role of genetic resources in breeding for climate change adaptation, highlighting the need for access to diverse germplasm to enhance breeders’ capacity to respond to climate challenges (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
• CGIAR promoted climate-smart agriculture through the Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC) to enhance resilience to extreme weather events (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
• CGIAR developed climate risk profiles for Mindanao, Luzon, and Visayas, identifying vulnerabilities and risks to key agricultural commodities and proposing on-farm adaptation options (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/113513, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/113512, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/113568).
Philippines.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched the greenhouse gas mitigation potential of Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) for rice production, demonstrating a significant decrease in CH4 emissions (https://doi.org/10.1029/2022jg006848).
• CGIAR assessed the poverty impacts of climate change mitigation policies, including forest carbon sequestration, in developing countries (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009).
Philippines.water: • CGIAR developed a spatial and temporal climate suitability assessment method for the water-saving technology 'Alternate Wetting and Drying' (AWD), demonstrating its potential to reduce water use by up to 30% (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0145268).

Zambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate risk management and agricultural adaptation in Zambia through various interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management strategies to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
- CGIAR evaluated the potential climate change impact on key rainfed cereals and the potential of cultivar adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
Zambia.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate risk management strategies to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers, focusing on stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR evaluated the potential climate change impact on key rainfed cereals (maize, millet, sorghum, and wheat) and the potential of cultivar adaptation to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
• CGIAR explored the relationship between farming diversity and food security, highlighting the importance of agricultural diversification as an adaptation strategy (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
• CGIAR conducted a cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart agriculture options, combining soil and water conservation management practices, improved varieties, and cereal-legume crop species (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation opportunities and challenges from a water-energy-food (WEF) nexus perspective, recommending strategies to build resilient communities (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR identified prioritized climate-smart agricultural practices adopted among smallholder farmers, assessing their economic feasibility (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, using adapted improved maize varieties (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed the role of plant breeding in adapting crops to climate changes, exploring the use of genetic diversity for adaptation (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
Zambia.mitigation: • CGIAR assessed the impact of intensifying or expanding cereal cropping in sub-Saharan Africa on greenhouse gas emissions and food security (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14783).
Zambia.water:
• CGIAR developed a long-term daily satellite-based rainfall dataset for operational monitoring in Africa, including Zambia (https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts on water resources and water management, exploring adaptation opportunities (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).

Mexico.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation strategies in Mexico through various innovative approaches. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions for smallholder farmers (Source 1). 
- CGIAR developed adaptation strategies for coffee communities in the Sierra Madre de Chiapas (Source 2).
Mexico.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers, reducing adverse impacts of climate-related risk (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR developed a climate change adaptation strategy for coffee communities in the Sierra Madre de Chiapas, focusing on sustainable land use practices and forest conservation (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9186-5).
• CGIAR created an integrated framework to assess vulnerability to climate change and develop adaptation strategies for coffee growers in Mesoamerica (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463).
• CGIAR evaluated the future of coffee and cocoa agroforestry in a warmer Mesoamerica, proposing agroforestry system transformation as an adaptation measure (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7).
• CGIAR used multiclass classification of agro-ecological zones to understand climate change impacts on arabica coffee and identify long-term adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0140490).
• CGIAR assessed the vulnerability of traditional maize seed systems in Mexico to climate change, highlighting the need for adapted planting material and improved seed-supply chains (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1103373108).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates to understand coupled impacts of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR examined gender and human development opportunities and trade-offs related to promoting conservation agriculture as part of a climate-smart food production system (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-013-0290-8).
• CGIAR reviewed climate-related migration in the Central American Dry Corridor, identifying adaptation strategies and the climate-security-migration nexus (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03549-6).
Mexico.mitigation: • CGIAR provided an overview of recent, ongoing, and planned restoration initiatives in Latin America and the Caribbean, highlighting their potential for climate change mitigation through forest landscape restoration (https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510).

United States.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient water management and agricultural adaptation in the United States through innovative modeling and policy assessments. Specifically:
United States.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined the adaptability of California's water supply system to long-term climatic and demographic changes, highlighting the system's ability to adapt through groundwater storage and water transfers (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-006-9079-5).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on walnut pests in California, projecting increased pest pressure due to temperature changes (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2010.02227.x).
• CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to evaluate responses to climate change, emphasizing the importance of ensemble modeling approaches (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411).
• CGIAR modeled the effect of a heat wave on maize production in the USA, quantifying the biophysical impact of weather extremes and future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2014.07.002).
United States.mitigation: • CGIAR researched the benefits and tradeoffs of reduced tillage and manure application methods in a corn silage system, focusing on greenhouse gas emissions and soil carbon losses (https://doi.org/10.1002/jeq2.20125).
United States.water:
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of human interventions and climate change on the terrestrial water cycle, identifying significant effects in certain river basins (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222475110).
• CGIAR estimated the impacts of climate warming on California water availability under various future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1752-1688.2005.tb03783.x).
• CGIAR explored the impacts of water rights and hydroclimate risk on irrigation decisions under water supply uncertainty (https://doi.org/10.1002/2016wr019779).
• CGIAR projected agricultural nutrient loadings to the freshwater environment, highlighting the role of climate change and socioeconomic change (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8148).

Nigeria.adaptation:

Indonesia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change research in Indonesia through mitigation and adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed carbon sequestration and biomass carbon storage on agricultural land (Source_1). 
- CGIAR examined cross-level interactions in climate change mitigation and adaptation policy processes in land use sectors (Source_2).
Indonesia.adaptation:
• CGIAR re-conceptualized Climate Policy Integration to minimize trade-offs and exploit synergies in the land use sector of Indonesia (Source_3).
• CGIAR identified long-term adaptation strategies for arabica coffee production under climate change (Source_4).

• CGIAR studied the potential effects of climate change on potato late blight risk in Indonesia (Source_6).

• CGIAR identified environmental refuges for Borneo's orang-utans by integrating bioclimatic models with projected deforestation (Source_8).
Indonesia.mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the role of trees on agricultural land and their significance for carbon sequestration globally, including Indonesia (Source_1).
• CGIAR evaluated peat carbon stock in Indonesia using national peatland maps and highlighted uncertainties and potential losses from conversion (Source_5).
• CGIAR quantified the organic carbon concentration and soil physical properties in cocoa-based agroforestry systems in Sulawesi, Indonesia (Source_6).

Guatemala.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation strategies in Guatemala through various research initiatives. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed an integrated framework for assessing vulnerability to climate change and identifying adaptation strategies for coffee growers (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463). 
- CGIAR assessed the future of coffee and cocoa agroforestry in a warmer Mesoamerica, identifying adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7).
Guatemala.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed an integrated framework for assessing vulnerability to climate change and developing adaptation strategies for coffee growing families in Mesoamerica (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0088463).
• CGIAR assessed the suitability of replacing coffee with cocoa and integrating trees in agroforestry systems to ameliorate abiotic stress (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7).
• CGIAR explored the role of genetic resources in breeding for climate change adaptation, focusing on traits for climate challenges (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
• CGIAR demonstrated grassroots approaches to collective action, conflict prevention, and social-ecological resilience in small-scale agriculture (https://doi.org/10.5751/es-10250-230305).
• CGIAR mapped climate change adaptive capacity and vulnerability of smallholder agricultural livelihoods in Central America (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR reviewed climate-related migration and the climate-security-migration nexus in the Central American Dry Corridor (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03549-6).
• CGIAR mapped adaptive capacity and smallholder agriculture, applying expert knowledge at the landscape scale (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2).
Guatemala.mitigation: • CGIAR researched land restoration in Latin America and the Caribbean, focusing on forest landscape restoration and its potential for climate change mitigation (https://doi.org/10.3390/f10060510).

Nepal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and water management in Nepal through innovative adaptation strategies and hydrological assessments. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the impact of heat stress on maize and the benefits of heat-tolerant varieties (Source 1). 
- CGIAR identified labor-saving climate-smart agriculture technologies for women farmers in high climatic risk areas (Source 2).
Nepal.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of current and future heat stress on maize and the potential benefits of heat-tolerant varieties in South Asia, including Nepal (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
• CGIAR identified potential labor-saving climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies for women farmers in high climatic risk areas in Nepal, such as direct seeded rice, system of rice intensification (SRI), laser land leveling (LLL), and green manuring (GM) (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-018-2350-8).
• CGIAR explored the links between access to weather information, household assets, and participation in social institutions with the likelihood of farmers making farm-associated changes, such as adopting improved crop varieties and land management practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011).
• CGIAR analyzed how public breeders in developing countries, including Nepal, are responding to climate challenges through the use of plant genetic resources and the challenges they face in accessing germplasm (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
• CGIAR characterized the governance and leadership patterns within agricultural development policy networks that connect organizations working on climate resilience and food security activities in South Asia, including Nepal (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2018.12.015).
• CGIAR documented local adaptations in the Upper Koshi, Nepal, including changing agricultural practices and water harvesting and management to cope with reduced precipitation and irregular rainfall patterns (https://doi.org/10.1080/21513732.2015.1027793).
• CGIAR identified adaptation strategies at the household, community, and landscape levels in the Panchase Mountain Ecological Region of Nepal, including livelihood diversification and changes in cropping patterns (https://doi.org/10.3390/environments5030042).
Nepal.water:
• CGIAR simulated the current and future hydrological system of the Chamelia Watershed in Western Nepal to assess streamflow response to projected future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.09.053).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on water resources in the Karnali River Basin, including irrigation water requirements for paddy and wheat, and the implications for water, energy, and agriculture sectors (https://doi.org/10.2166/nh.2025.193).

Malawi.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation strategies in Malawi through diverse agricultural and institutional interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
- CGIAR explored agricultural diversification as a strategy for achieving food security (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
- CGIAR conducted a cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart agriculture options balancing gender and technology (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013).

• CGIAR conducted a cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart agriculture options balancing gender and technology (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR analyzed temporary migration responses to local temperature and precipitation anomalies in Eastern Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2019.104704).
• CGIAR presented a multi-dimensional framework to evaluate climate-smart agricultural practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR investigated the potential impacts of climate change on root, tuber, and banana crops in Central Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246).
• CGIAR assessed gender-based differences in access, use, and benefits from rural climate services (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
• CGIAR promoted fodder trees for improving livestock productivity and smallholder livelihoods in Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008).
Malawi.water:
• CGIAR conducted a cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart agriculture options including soil and water conservation management practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Burkina Faso.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation in Burkina Faso through various strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) technologies, including agroforestry and soil and water conservation, to manage climate-related risks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199).
- CGIAR assessed the potential climate change impact on key rainfed cereals and the potential of cultivar adaptation to mitigate these impacts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).

• CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions, including stress-adapted crop germplasm and conservation agriculture, to stabilize production and incomes (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR explored agricultural diversification as a strategy for achieving food security, highlighting the relationship between farming diversity and food security (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
• CGIAR analyzed the role of climate-smart agriculture in transforming food systems under climate change pressure (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084305).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for the livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined the pathways through which agroforestry affects human health and food security in Sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017).
• CGIAR studied smallholder farmer cropping decisions related to climate variability and the drivers of agricultural change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR estimated farmers’ willingness to pay for climate information services to improve adaptive capacity in Northern Burkina Faso (https://doi.org/10.3390/su10030611).

China.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate research in China through diverse innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed carbon sequestration through agroforestry (Source 1).
- CGIAR evaluated GHG mitigation strategies (Source 2).
- CGIAR analyzed nitrogen use efficiencies in cereal systems (Source 3).
- CGIAR projected crop failure rates under climate change (Source 4).
- CGIAR studied rangeland vulnerability to climate change (Source 5).
- CGIAR explored genetic resources for climate adaptation (Source 6).
- CGIAR synthesized soil carbon management in grasslands (Source 7).
- CGIAR examined hydrological responses to land-cover changes (Source 8).
- CGIAR analyzed hydropower versus irrigation dynamics (Source 9).
- CGIAR investigated rice production adaptation through land use shifts (Source 10).
China.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed adaptation options for wheat in Northeast China, highlighting socio-economic and biophysical measures (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/5/3/034012).
• CGIAR analyzed global rangeland production systems' vulnerability to climate change, identifying adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR explored the role of genetic resources in breeding for climate change adaptation in developing countries (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
• CGIAR investigated rice production adaptation through land use shifts in Northeast China (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aafa55).
China.mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the role of trees on agricultural land for carbon sequestration and climate change mitigation (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
• CGIAR evaluated the implications of climate change mitigation in the land use sector for agricultural production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa8c83).
• CGIAR synthesized the impacts of land use conversion and improved management practices on soil organic carbon in China's grasslands (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2011.06.002).
• CGIAR analyzed the competition between hydropower and irrigation for water availability, highlighting climate change mitigation implications (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa5f3f).
China.water:
• CGIAR analyzed nitrogen use efficiencies and environmental adversity in cereal-based systems, particularly in China (https://doi.org/10.1016/bs.agron.2020.05.006).
• CGIAR examined the response of hydrological processes to land-cover and climate changes in Southwest China (https://doi.org/10.1002/hyp.7233).
• CGIAR assessed the global patterns of hydropower versus irrigation and their implications for water productivity (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aa5f3f).

Honduras.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation in Honduras through various innovative approaches. Specifically:
- CGIAR modeled climate change impacts on smallholder agriculture and ecosystems to inform adaptation responses (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y).
- CGIAR assessed the suitability of cocoa and agroforestry systems as alternatives to coffee production under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7).
- CGIAR co-designed climate-smart farming systems with local stakeholders to achieve large-scale change (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2019.00037).
- CGIAR mapped climate change adaptive capacity and vulnerability to support adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1792-0).
- CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under future climates to target management practices (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
- CGIAR leveraged expert input to map adaptive capacity of smallholder farmers for adaptation planning (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2).
- CGIAR investigated differentiated access to climate-smart agricultural interventions among female farmers (https://doi.org/10.3390/su131910951).
- CGIAR estimated yield changes in drybeans due to climate change to identify high-impact spots (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2).
- CGIAR developed a gamified method for farmer-participatory priority setting in plant breeding (https://doi.org/10.1080/15427528.2017.1303801).
- CGIAR analyzed institutional networks to improve coordination of climate-change adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2024.100664).

Cameroon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation strategies in Cameroon through various research initiatives. Specifically:
- CGIAR analyzed the vulnerability of cocoa to climate change and proposed adaptation strategies such as shade trees and breeding programs (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024).
- CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for agriculture, including climate-smart agriculture to improve resilience (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
- CGIAR examined the impacts of COVID-19 lockdowns on food security and proposed policies to address food security challenges (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).
- CGIAR assessed the potential impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, suggesting measures to adapt maize to future climate conditions (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
- CGIAR evaluated the potential cocoa productivity responses to climate change and identified areas where productivity could be maintained or increased (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2025.110393).
- CGIAR reviewed trade-offs and synergies of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
- CGIAR discussed a regional approach to climate change adaptation for cocoa, dividing the region into adaptation zones (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y).
- CGIAR highlighted the resilience of indigenous food systems in the face of climate change (https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en).
- CGIAR mapped the vulnerability of priority tree species to climate change and proposed conservation and restoration strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).

Nicaragua.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation strategies in Nicaragua through innovative research and policy frameworks. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management interventions to stabilize production and incomes for smallholder farmers (Source_1). 
- CGIAR developed crop variety management for climate adaptation supported by citizen science (Source_2). 
- CGIAR quantified the impact of climate change on coffee production and developed an adaptation framework (Source_3). 
- CGIAR developed a Climate Risk Vulnerability Assessment to inform sub-national decision-making (Source_4). 
- CGIAR reviewed national and local adaptation responses to climate change impacts on smallholder agriculture (Source_5). 
- CGIAR assessed vulnerability to climate change and developed adaptation strategies for coffee-growing families (Source_6). 
- CGIAR assessed the suitability of cocoa and agroforestry systems as alternatives to coffee production under climate change (Source_7). 
- CGIAR mapped climate change adaptive capacity and vulnerability of smallholder agricultural livelihoods (Source_8). 
- CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates (Source_9).

Cambodia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate risk management and rural poverty reduction in Cambodia through various adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the role of climate-risk management interventions in reducing rural poverty (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019). 
- CGIAR reviewed gender-responsive rural climate services to address inequalities in access and use (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
Cambodia.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the role of climate-risk management interventions in stabilizing production and incomes, protecting assets, and improving farmer welfare (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR reviewed gender-responsive rural climate services to address inequalities in access and use of climate information (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
• CGIAR developed multi-factor, multi-state, multi-model scenarios to explore food and climate futures for Southeast Asia, including Cambodia (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envsoft.2016.05.008).
• CGIAR examined the impact of climate change on rice production and evaluated adaptation options for food security in the lower Mekong Basin (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
• CGIAR reviewed the role of migration as an adaptation strategy to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
• CGIAR provided pest-smart interventions and early warning systems to alleviate problems due to pests and diseases under climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537).
• CGIAR developed a step-by-step process for establishing Climate-Smart Villages (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107725).
• CGIAR reviewed projections of demand and supply of livestock-derived foods and the implications for livestock sector management under climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101135).
Cambodia.water:
• CGIAR examined the impact of climate change on rice production and evaluated adaptation options for food security in the lower Mekong Basin (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
• CGIAR reviewed the current status and trends in water management in the Greater Mekong Subregion and assessed likely impacts of climate change on water resources (https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212).

Niger.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies in Niger through various innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture technologies for adaptation and water conservation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199).
- CGIAR examined adaptation strategies and their impacts on household income and food security (https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847).
Niger.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the potential climate change impact on key rainfed cereals and the potential of cultivar adaptation in Niger (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
• CGIAR explored the relationship between farming diversity and food security, highlighting agricultural diversification as a strategy for achieving food security (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
• CGIAR identified prioritized climate-smart agricultural practices adopted among smallholder farmers in Niger, focusing on improved seed, good agricultural practices, and conservation agriculture practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2022.e09228).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy developments for the livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in West Africa, including Niger (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR analyzed the pathways through which agroforestry affects food and nutrition security, infectious disease prevalence, non-communicable diseases, and human migration in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Niger (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017).
• CGIAR examined smallholder farmer cropping decisions related to climate variability across multiple regions, including Niger, focusing on access to weather information, household assets, and participation in social institutions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2013.12.011).
• CGIAR examined the relationship between climate-smart agriculture practices, land productivity, and food security in West Africa, including Niger (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697).
Niger.mitigation: • CGIAR researched the relationship between climate-smart agriculture practices and the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions in West Africa, including Niger (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2023.102697).
Niger.water: • CGIAR developed a long-term daily satellite-based rainfall dataset for operational monitoring in Africa, including Niger, providing temporally consistent historic and near-real-time daily rainfall information (https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2017.63).

Myanmar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and food security in Myanmar through various adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the role of agroforestry in carbon sequestration (Source 1). 
- CGIAR implemented climate risk management interventions for rural poverty reduction (Source 2).
Myanmar.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate-related risk management and rural poverty reduction through stress-adapted crop germplasm, conservation agriculture, and diversified production systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR analyzed climate action for food security in South Asia, focusing on adaptation initiatives and climate-resilient agricultural value chains (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• CGIAR examined trans-disciplinary responses to climate change, including the Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC) for resilience to extreme weather events (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
• CGIAR developed a Myanmar Climate-Smart Agriculture Strategy to achieve sustainable agricultural development through climate-resilient and sustainable agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091).
• CGIAR studied migration as an adaptation strategy to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes, highlighting the complex outcomes of migration (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
• CGIAR investigated the impacts of climate change on local food systems in Myanmar, emphasizing the need for climate adaptation in food production (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.indic.2022.100170).
• CGIAR analyzed the community-level impacts of climate-smart agriculture interventions on food security and dietary diversity in climate-smart villages in Myanmar (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli9110166).
Myanmar.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched the role of agroforestry in climate change mitigation, focusing on carbon sequestration and biomass carbon on agricultural land (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
• CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture and food systems that reduce poverty and hunger, including strategies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions (https://doi.org/10.1787/50075aa6-en).
• CGIAR analyzed the role of agriculture in nationally determined contributions to the Paris Agreement, focusing on emission reduction commitments and greenhouse gas reduction (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• CGIAR developed a Myanmar Climate-Smart Agriculture Strategy that aims to promote sustainable agriculture, food security, and climate change mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69091).
Myanmar.water: • CGIAR reviewed the impacts of climate change on water resources in the Greater Mekong Subregion, focusing on water management strategies to safeguard food production (https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212).

Pakistan.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and water resource management in Pakistan through innovative adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically:
Pakistan.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of heat stress on maize and the benefits of heat-tolerant varieties in South Asia, including Pakistan, to help farmers adapt to climate change impacts (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
• CGIAR analyzed the role of agriculture in nationally determined contributions to the Paris Agreement, focusing on adaptation initiatives and climate-resilient agricultural value chains in South Asia (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• CGIAR developed a methodology to identify and map climate-agriculture-gender inequality hotspots, highlighting the need to address structural barriers to gender equality in agri-food systems (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1197809).
• CGIAR explored trans-disciplinary responses to climate change, including the Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC) in South and Southeast Asia, to enhance resilience to extreme weather events (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change scenarios on rice and wheat crops in the Indo-Gangetic Plains, projecting yield reductions and the need for adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.54386/jam.v16i1.1480).
Pakistan.mitigation:
• CGIAR examined the potential of alternative agricultural practices to reduce net greenhouse gas emissions and maintain yields despite extreme weather, including agroforestry and emission reduction strategies (https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1217941).
• CGIAR analyzed the Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) of South Asian nations, assessing their commitments to global mitigation efforts and domestic adaptation initiatives (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
Pakistan.water:
• CGIAR analyzed the hydrological response under CMIP6 climate projections in the Astore River Basin, Pakistan, revealing changes in streamflow and the need for adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11629-022-7872-x).
• CGIAR evaluated the impact of climate change on consumptive water use in the Lower Chenab Canal area of the Indus basin, highlighting the need for better water allocation plans (https://doi.org/10.2166/nh.2016.102).

Congo.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and water management in Congo through innovative research and policy recommendations. Specifically:
Congo.adaptation:
• CGIAR explored agricultural diversification as a strategy for achieving food security in Africa, highlighting the relationship between farming diversity and food security, and the diversification potential of African agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14158).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation opportunities and challenges in southern Africa from a water-energy-food (WEF) nexus perspective, recommending strategies to develop cross-sectoral sustainable measures aimed at building resilient communities (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR analyzed the implications of COVID-19 pandemic movement restrictions on food security in Sub-Saharan Africa, emphasizing the need for better policies to confront the challenge of reducing hunger post-pandemic (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa, using adapted improved maize varieties and bioeconomic models (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR investigated the potential impacts of climate change on root, tuber, and banana crops in the Great Lakes Region of Central Africa, identifying climate-proof variety types and developing spatially explicit recommendations for adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103246).
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, highlighting potential trade-offs and synergies related to decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR mapped the vulnerability of 100 socio-ecologically important priority tree species in Central Africa to climate change and other anthropogenic threats, proposing a spatially explicit strategy to guide restoration and conservation actions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
• CGIAR optimized the yam crop calendar for various South-Kivu agro-ecological zones in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, adapting to changing climate conditions and recommending soil water conservation practices (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0309775).
• CGIAR explored vulnerability and adaptation strategies to climate change of local communities in Bukavu in DR Congo, identifying climate-related risks and barriers to adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1177/1070496514536395).
Congo.water:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa, highlighting climate change impacts on water resources and recommending a well-coordinated and integrated approach to build resilient systems (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined climate change impacts on maize production and food security, using bioeconomic models to estimate the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts under different climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, using models that consider detailed water-agriculture linkages and general equilibrium approaches (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

• CGIAR synthesized experiences of Climate-Smart Villages to identify pathways for scaling climate-smart agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115833).

South Africa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and water management in South Africa through innovative monitoring and adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed near real-time agricultural monitoring systems for crop management (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.11.007).
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on maize production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
South Africa.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined gender-responsive rural climate services to enhance adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
• CGIAR predicted maize yield under push-pull technology in response to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2023.108511).
• CGIAR studied smallholder farmers' adaptation strategies to climate variability (https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture10090410).
• CGIAR promoted fodder trees to improve livestock productivity and smallholder livelihoods (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008).
• CGIAR analyzed trade-offs and synergies of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
South Africa.water:
• CGIAR investigated the impacts of climate change, policy, and the Water-Energy-Food nexus on hydropower development (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.renene.2017.10.030).
• CGIAR analyzed rainfall variability and trends to inform water resource management (https://doi.org/10.3390/rs14010096).

Egypt.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized sustainable development and climate resilience in Egypt through policy frameworks, gender-responsive climate services, and agricultural adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR introduced a national-scale design framework to balance economic, social, and environmental goals for sustainable development (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-32415-9). 
- CGIAR assessed gender-based differences in access to rural climate services to enhance gender equality and climate information responsiveness (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
Egypt.adaptation:
• CGIAR identified urgent adaptation investments needed for Egypt’s agriculture sector due to climate change impacts (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318).
• CGIAR highlighted the importance of climate-resilience policies and investments for sustaining productivity and food security in Egypt’s agriculture sector (https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896294189).
• CGIAR explored CRISPR genome editing to develop climate-resilient crops and improve crop quality and yield (https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003382102-5).
• CGIAR examined a range of climate change adaptation approaches to counteract agricultural productivity declines in Egypt (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321).
• CGIAR conducted a water profitability analysis to improve food security and climate resilience in the Egyptian Nile Delta (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli10020017).
• CGIAR developed an ingredient database for the formulation of sustainable, local fish feeds to support resilient agrifood systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Y1HVFF).
Egypt.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched policy portfolios that achieve efficient mixes of poverty and inequality reduction, economic growth, and climate change mitigation (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-32415-9).
• CGIAR emphasized the need for global cooperation to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions to reduce adverse climate change impacts on Egypt’s society (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318).
Egypt.water:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of water management and high water-consuming cereals on climate change adaptation strategies for Egypt’s agricultural sector (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321).
• CGIAR conducted a water profitability analysis to establish a baseline and assess the net return per unit of water of main crops in the Egyptian Nile Delta (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli10020017).

Ecuador.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change research in Ecuador through various innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR examined hydrologic impacts of afforestation/reforestation under the Clean Development Mechanism (Source 1).
- CGIAR modeled coffee suitability and pollinator distributions under climate change (Source 2).
- CGIAR analyzed agroecology's contributions to climate change adaptation and mitigation (Source 3).
- CGIAR explored climate change policies related to rural development (Source 4).
- CGIAR studied the design of incentive-based conservation policies like REDD+ (Source 5).
- CGIAR proposed methods to evaluate climate change impacts on food and nutrition security (Source 6).
- CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on cocoa production (Source 7).
- CGIAR conducted participatory resilience workshops with Indigenous knowledge systems (Source 8).
- CGIAR provided guidelines for implementing Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (Source 9).
- CGIAR surveyed adoption of improved modern rice varieties and management practices (Source 10).

• CGIAR conducted participatory resilience workshops with Indigenous knowledge systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/105603).

Benin.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and water management in Benin through targeted research and adaptation strategies. Specifically:
Benin.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in West Africa, including Benin, to promote climate-smart agriculture and improve farming systems' resilience (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR analyzed the implications of COVID-19 lockdowns during planting seasons on food security in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Benin, and proposed policies to reduce hunger post-pandemic (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Benin, using bioeconomic models to estimate future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR assessed gender-based differences in access to rural climate services in Benin, identifying pathways to make climate services more responsive to the needs of rural women and men (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Benin, highlighting trade-offs and synergies in decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR analyzed the impact of climate change on cropping patterns in central Benin, combining hydrological modeling and scenario analysis to assess future food security challenges (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642).
• CGIAR evaluated the economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Benin, under different adaptation scenarios using partial and general equilibrium models (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR studied the impacts of climate change on water resources in the Volta River Basin, including Benin, to guide adaptation and mitigation strategies for regional socioeconomic development (https://doi.org/10.5194/hess-26-1481-2022).
• CGIAR investigated heat stress conditions for dairy cattle production in West Africa, including Benin, under current and future climatic conditions, calling for improved adaptive capacity (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2).
Benin.water:
• CGIAR analyzed the impact of climate change on cropping patterns in central Benin, combining hydrological modeling and scenario analysis to assess future food security challenges (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0192642).
• CGIAR evaluated the economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Benin, under different adaptation scenarios using partial and general equilibrium models (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR studied the impacts of climate change on water resources in the Volta River Basin, including Benin, to guide adaptation and mitigation strategies for regional socioeconomic development (https://doi.org/10.5194/hess-26-1481-2022).
• CGIAR presented a comprehensive modeling framework integrating hydrological models and climate change scenarios to anticipate future water resource challenges in the Volta River Basin, including Benin (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhydrol.2023.130092).

Sri Lanka.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and adaptation strategies in Sri Lanka through innovative research and policy frameworks. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed frameworks for prioritizing adaptation approaches in agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1219441110).
- CGIAR assessed the spatial variability of rainfall trends to inform water resource management (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijgi10020084).
- CGIAR evaluated the impact of heat stress on maize and the benefits of heat-tolerant varieties (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
Sri Lanka.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed a framework for prioritizing adaptation approaches in agriculture, including incremental vs. transformative adaptation pathways (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1219441110).
• CGIAR analyzed spatial variability of rainfall trends to inform drought and flood management (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijgi10020084).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of heat stress on maize and the potential benefits of heat-tolerant varieties (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
• CGIAR investigated the impact of climate change on paddy farming in the Village Tank Cascade Systems (https://doi.org/10.3390/su15129271).
• CGIAR developed a pilot-level climate change vulnerability index for water resources and agriculture (https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.211).
• CGIAR analyzed the role of agriculture in nationally determined contributions to the Paris Agreement (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• CGIAR studied the potential distribution of crop wild relatives under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crsust.2021.100092).
• CGIAR explored trans-disciplinary responses to climate change in rice-based systems (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
• CGIAR tested bundled climate-smart agricultural solutions for smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2023.1145147).
• CGIAR developed a country profile on climate-smart agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69548).

Morocco.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and agricultural adaptation in Morocco through innovative research and policy interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate risk management strategies for smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
- CGIAR explored the role of genetic resources in breeding climate-resilient crops (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
- CGIAR analyzed the impact of ENSO teleconnections on Moroccan agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jastp.2024.106386).
- CGIAR evaluated the impact of climate change on Argan tree distribution (https://doi.org/10.1007/s40808-024-02077-1).
- CGIAR engaged youth in climate-smart agriculture initiatives (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92979).
- CGIAR validated adaptation measures for truffle farming (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723).
- CGIAR developed indicators for tracking Climate Smart Investment Plans (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KO3LT9).
- CGIAR contributed to the development of climate-smart agriculture investment plans (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262).
Morocco.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate risk management strategies for smallholder farmers, focusing on production technologies and institutional interventions to stabilize production and incomes (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2018.01.019).
• CGIAR explored the role of genetic resources in breeding climate-resilient crops, highlighting the need for access to diverse germplasm (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
• CGIAR analyzed the impact of ENSO teleconnections on Moroccan agriculture, particularly on drought severity and wheat yields (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jastp.2024.106386).
• CGIAR evaluated the impact of climate change on Argan tree distribution, emphasizing the need for proactive adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1007/s40808-024-02077-1).
• CGIAR engaged youth in climate-smart agriculture initiatives, addressing challenges and opportunities in the context of climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92979).
• CGIAR validated adaptation measures for truffle farming, focusing on improved livestock management practices and water-efficient horticulture systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723).
• CGIAR developed indicators for tracking Climate Smart Investment Plans, including conservation agriculture in Morocco (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KO3LT9).
• CGIAR contributed to the development of climate-smart agriculture investment plans, highlighting the need for adapting to climate change risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262).
Morocco.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched the institutional barriers to agricultural mitigation in Morocco, highlighting the challenges in engaging smallholder farmers in mitigation projects (https://doi.org/10.2499/capriwp107).
• CGIAR contributed to the development of climate-smart agriculture investment plans, which include reducing greenhouse gas emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262).
Morocco.water:
• CGIAR analyzed the impact of climate change on agricultural water demand in the Souss Basin, using a decision support system to understand water resource planning challenges (https://doi.org/10.3389/frwa.2024.1270078).
• CGIAR validated adaptation measures for truffle farming, promoting water-efficient horticulture systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65723).

Thailand.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agricultural systems in Thailand through trans-disciplinary networks, adaptation strategies, and mitigation technologies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR fostered the Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC) to enhance resilience to extreme weather events (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035). 
- CGIAR evaluated adaptation options like changing planting dates and supplementary irrigation for rice production (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
Thailand.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined trans-disciplinary responses to climate change through networks like the Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC) to address reduced crop yields and extreme weather events (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
• CGIAR assessed adaptation options such as changing planting dates and supplementary irrigation to offset negative impacts on rainfed rice yields (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-011-0154-z).
• CGIAR explored migration as an adaptation strategy to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes, highlighting its role in risk reduction (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
• CGIAR developed guidelines for establishing Climate-Smart Villages to enhance food security (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107725).
• CGIAR analyzed long-term changes in rainfall distribution in Northeast Thailand and discussed cropping systems adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006).
Thailand.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched the poverty impacts of climate change mitigation policies, including forest carbon sequestration in non-Annex regions (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035009).
• CGIAR assessed the climatic suitability of the alternate wetting and drying (AWD) water management technique for mitigating methane emissions in paddy fields (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.575823).
• CGIAR explored enabling policies for circular economy and climate change mitigation in rice production, focusing on methane-reducing practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/163419).
• CGIAR investigated mitigation options for greenhouse gas emissions from agricultural lands in Southeast Asia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003).
Thailand.water:
• CGIAR evaluated the impact of climate change on water resources and food production in the Greater Mekong Subregion (https://doi.org/10.5337/2010.212).
• CGIAR assessed the climatic suitability of the alternate wetting and drying (AWD) technique for water management in rice cultivation (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.575823).
• CGIAR analyzed long-term changes in rainfall distribution and their implications for water management in Northeast Thailand (https://doi.org/10.1051/cagri/2017006).

Syria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and agricultural adaptation in Syria through targeted research and policy interventions. Specifically: 
- CGIAR evaluated the vulnerability of native rangeland plant species to climate change (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed the economic impacts of climate change on Syria's agricultural sector and households (Source 2).
Syria.adaptation:
• CGIAR evaluated the vulnerability of native rangeland plant species to climate change, highlighting the need for grazing management and mitigation measures (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z).
• CGIAR analyzed the economic impacts of climate change in Syria, showing welfare losses across rural and urban households and the need for adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1142/s2010007813500024).
• CGIAR documented the devastating impacts of climate change on rural producers in Syria, emphasizing the need for adaptation and resilience-building (https://doi.org/10.1596/978-0-8213-9846-3).
• CGIAR developed participatory plant breeding programs to enhance agricultural biodiversity and address farmers’ needs, including the rediscovery of a drought-tolerant wheat variety (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36078).
• CGIAR implemented decentralized-participatory plant breeding to fit crops to marginal environments like barley in Syria (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36077).
• CGIAR proposed climate-resilient agriculture as a strategy for Syria’s post-conflict recovery, emphasizing targeted investments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173986).
Syria.water:
• CGIAR assessed the potential of supplemental irrigation to reduce inter-annual variability in wheat yields in northern Syria (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/42086).
• CGIAR mapped agricultural water productivity for irrigated areas in the Euphrates-Tigris basin, identifying options for improving water management (hdl:20.500.11766.1/CUZ56W).
• CGIAR determined agricultural water productivity for rainfed areas in the Euphrates-Tigris basin, providing insights for water management (hdl:20.500.11766.1/D1D3GI).

Sudan.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in Sudan through targeted research and adaptation strategies. Specifically:
Sudan.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, using bioeconomic models to estimate future yield reductions and adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers, identifying trade-offs and synergies in decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR applied the APSIM model to assess the impacts of projected climate change on sorghum varieties, enhancing adaptive capacity through improved management practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685).
• CGIAR analyzed the widespread impact of the parasitic weed Rhamphicarpa fistulosa on rice production, advocating for integrated weed management to reduce yield losses (https://doi.org/10.1111/wre.12129).
• CGIAR discussed the potential impact of climatic change on vector abundance and tick-borne pathogens, focusing on anthropogenic cattle movement in South Sudan (https://doi.org/10.1079/9781789249637.0070).
• CGIAR identified how climate variability exacerbates root causes of conflict in Sudan, particularly through reduced agricultural production and food insecurity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128061).
