Costa Rica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in Costa Rica through innovative adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate change threats to cocoa and coffee agroforestry systems (Source 1).

- CGIAR modeled the impact of climate change on coffee suitability and pollinator richness (Source 3).

- CGIAR mapped adaptive capacity of smallholder coffee farmers (Source 5).
- CGIAR identified the role of silvopastoral systems in climate change mitigation (Source 6).
- CGIAR formulated climate-adapted agriculture strategies for the SICA region (Source 7).
- CGIAR evaluated the potential of biological corridors for plant dispersal under climate change (Source 10).
Costa Rica.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed climate change threats to cocoa and coffee agroforestry systems, proposing agroforestry system transformation as an adaptation measure (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7).
• CGIAR explored the role of genetic resources in breeding for climate change adaptation, highlighting challenges in accessing germplasm (https://doi.org/10.3390/plants9091129).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates, suggesting management practices like forest conservation and shade adjustment (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR developed high-resolution climate change projections for Central America, assessing impacts on precipitation, temperature, and water availability (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0193570).
• CGIAR mapped adaptive capacity of smallholder coffee farmers, using expert input to identify vulnerable communities for adaptation planning (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1810-2).
• CGIAR formulated climate-adapted agriculture strategies for the SICA region, involving various stakeholders in the process (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89328).
• CGIAR evaluated the role of biological corridors for facilitating plant dispersal under climate change in Mesoamerica (https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672).
Costa Rica.mitigation:

• CGIAR evaluated the NAMA Ganadería eco-competitiva initiative in Costa Rica, aiming to reduce greenhouse gas emissions through changes in livestock production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79962).
Costa Rica.water:

Argentina.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate research in Argentina through targeted studies on mitigation, adaptation, and water. Specifically:
Argentina.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on future crop yields and yield gaps in Argentina, providing guidelines for data requirements and resilience management (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2015.03.004).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates in Latin America, including Argentina, to understand the coupled impacts of climate change on crop suitability and pollination (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR predicted the distribution of plant species in southern South America, identifying hotspots of genetic diversity threatened by climate change, particularly in northern Patagonia, Argentina (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-023-02770-0).
• CGIAR examined migration as an adaptation strategy due to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes, highlighting the multifaceted outcomes of migration in response to climate vulnerabilities (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
Argentina.mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the role of trees on agricultural land in Argentina, highlighting their significance for carbon sequestration and the impact of biomass carbon changes (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
• CGIAR quantified proximate drivers of deforestation and related carbon losses in South America, including northern Argentina, to support the development of national REDD+ interventions (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/10/12/124004).
• CGIAR identified the capacity of silvopastoral systems (SPS) in Argentina to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, increase carbon stocks, and adapt to climate change, contributing to the Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-43063-3_2).
• CGIAR explored how well soil carbon concentration could be estimated in Argentina from existing climate, topography, and vegetation-cover data, aiming to improve soil carbon maps (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1466-8238.2012.00799.x).
• CGIAR researched climate change policies in Latin America, focusing on the mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests and adaptations to climate change in agriculture, including Argentina (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).
Argentina.water: • CGIAR analyzed changes in the South American monsoon system (SAMS) using gridded precipitation data and CMIP5 simulations, projecting increases in seasonal amplitudes and total monsoon precipitation over southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northern Argentina (https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-12-00412.1).

Somalia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and agricultural adaptation in Somalia through targeted research and policy analysis. Specifically: 
- CGIAR examined climate change impacts on maize production and food security (Source 1). 
- CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (Source 2).
Somalia.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, including yield reductions and adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers, highlighting trade-offs and synergies in decision-making (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR analyzed the economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture, evaluating adaptation scenarios to cope with climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR assessed the degree of awareness of climate-related security risks and climate peace opportunities in policy outputs (https://doi.org/10.3828/idpr.2023.9).
• CGIAR explored the nexus between climate change, agriculture, and international migration, focusing on the youth perspective (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278).
• CGIAR examined the interplay between climate, mobility, peace, and security in Somalia, advocating for sustainable agricultural practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173200).
• CGIAR quantified the implications of changing climate on the productivity and profitability of livestock smallholders (https://doi.org/10.1088/2976-601x/ad6fa0).
• CGIAR reviewed agricultural production systems in relation to food and nutrition security and climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995).
Somalia.mitigation: • CGIAR reviewed greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and climate-smart agriculture (CSA) in relation to livestock systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995).
Somalia.water:
• CGIAR analyzed the economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture, including water resources and adaptation scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR assessed current and projected changes in climate extremes and agro-climatic zones, including water stress and drought situations (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-025-05405-2).
• CGIAR advanced the use of gridded, online climate information for risk management, including agro-climatic variables (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92093).

Uzbekistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and water management in Uzbekistan through innovative research and adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed rangeland vulnerability and adaptation strategies for livestock production (Source 1). 
- CGIAR predicted soil salinity dynamics to improve irrigation management under climate change (Source 2).
Uzbekistan.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed rangeland vulnerability and adaptation strategies for livestock production, highlighting declines in vegetation biomass and increased variability in grazing availability (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR predicted soil salinity dynamics using statistical models to inform future management strategies for sustainable agriculture (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph19148794).
• CGIAR investigated smallholder adaptation strategies to address climate vulnerability and extreme events like droughts in rural Central Asia (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).
• CGIAR analyzed the impact of climate change on crop yields, distinguishing between spring and winter plantings, irrigated and rainfed crops (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134920).
• CGIAR explored improved feed and forage options to enhance resilience to climate change, including drought-resistant and salt-tolerant legume shrubs/trees (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132279).
• CGIAR examined the climate vulnerabilities of Central Asia's water, agriculture, and energy sectors, focusing on adaptive capacities and climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR studied the impacts of climate change on small household farms, particularly women's empowerment and household well-being (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179441).
Uzbekistan.mitigation:
• CGIAR analyzed greenhouse gas emissions, particularly methane from livestock, and the potential for improved feed and forage options to reduce environmental impacts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/132279).
• CGIAR examined the climate vulnerabilities of Central Asia's water, agriculture, and energy sectors, including the imperative for carbon neutrality by the middle of the century (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Uzbekistan.water:
• CGIAR investigated the impact of climate change on farm revenues and water use efficiencies in Western Uzbekistan, using a spatially explicit stochastic optimization model (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2016.05.004).
• CGIAR assessed the climate vulnerabilities of Central Asia's water sectors, using projections of river discharge under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR studied the impacts of climate change on water and energy scarcity, particularly in the context of small household farms (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179441).

Togo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and water management in Togo through targeted research and adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for agriculture in West Africa (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production (Source 2).
Togo.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy developments for agriculture in West Africa, focusing on climate-smart agriculture to improve resilience (Source 1).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, using adapted improved maize varieties (Source 2).
• CGIAR analyzed trade-offs and synergies of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa (Source 3).
• CGIAR evaluated adaptation scenarios to climate change for Sub-Saharan Africa, including doubling the irrigated area and increasing crop yields (Source 4).
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on hydrological processes in the Volta River Basin, highlighting the need for adaptation and mitigation strategies (Source 5).
• CGIAR studied heat stress conditions for dairy cattle production in West Africa under current and future climatic conditions (Source 7).
• CGIAR modeled future biome shifts and tree cover change in West Africa, accounting for human impact and climatic conditions (Source 8).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change adaptation on farm-level revenue among soybean farmers in Togo, considering gender-differentiated impacts (Source 9).
Togo.water:
• CGIAR conducted water accounting under climate change in the transboundary Volta River Basin, integrating hydrological models and climate change scenarios (Source 6).
• CGIAR analyzed trends in reference evapotranspiration and aridity index in Togo, highlighting the impact on rainfed agriculture (Source 10).

Central African Republic.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and agricultural adaptation in the Central African Republic through targeted research and policy assessments. Specifically: 
- CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (Source 1). 
- CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers, highlighting trade-offs and synergies (Source 2).
Central African Republic.adaptation:

Madagascar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and resilience in Madagascar through various strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR examined climate change impacts on maize production and food security, highlighting yield reductions and adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005). 
- CGIAR identified climate risk hotspots, including vulnerability to extreme rainfall and drought hazards in northern Madagascar (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109192).
Madagascar.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers, focusing on trade-offs and synergies in decision-making (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR assessed climate change vulnerability and resilience in the agricultural sector of the Union of the Comoros, situated between Mozambique and Madagascar (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78667).
• CGIAR analyzed the widespread impact of the parasitic weed Rhamphicarpa fistulosa on rice production in Africa, including Madagascar, and advocated for integrated weed management (https://doi.org/10.1111/wre.12129).
• CGIAR examined the food security threats in East Africa, including Madagascar, and explored adaptation measures to mitigate climate change impacts (https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292055).
• CGIAR reviewed adaptation strategies in rural Sub-Saharan Africa, including Madagascar, and assessed the incorporation of climate change adaptation in national development plans (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33403).
• CGIAR implemented a seed resilience strategy in Madagascar to strengthen farmers' capacity to adapt to climate change through better access to crop and varietal diversity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
Madagascar.water:
• CGIAR analyzed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus in southern Africa, including impacts on water resources and management in Madagascar (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture in Sub-Saharan Africa, including water management and adaptation scenarios in Madagascar (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

Canada.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and adaptation measures in Canada through various research initiatives. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on rangeland ecosystem services and adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995). 
- CGIAR investigated scaling up innovations in smallholder agriculture to address climate variability and socio-ecological dynamics (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.05.012).
Canada.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on rangeland ecosystem services and adaptation measures, highlighting the sensitivity of forage production in Africa and the need for cost-benefit analyses of adaptation policies (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995).
• CGIAR explored scaling up food security innovations in low-income rural environments, considering multiple stress factors such as climate variability and economic risks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.05.012).
• CGIAR reviewed G20 countries' recommendations for healthy and sustainable food system policies to address climate change effects and manage sustainability trade-offs (https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en).
• CGIAR proposed adaptation measures to mitigate negative effects of climate change on agricultural production, including research and development for new crop varieties and changes in irrigation technology (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR developed climate-yield models to identify climate risks and opportunities for potato production, enhancing supply-chain resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
• CGIAR implemented participatory action research in Climate-Smart Villages to address climate change challenges and enhance livelihoods and household resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123688).
• CGIAR reviewed methodologies and evidence on adaptations in the agri-food systems to measure the effectiveness of adaptation in reducing climate risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/135997).
• CGIAR promoted community-based adaptation processes and a portfolio of Climate-Smart Agriculture options to address water scarcity and other climate challenges (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123689).
Canada.mitigation:
• CGIAR explored improving emission quantification methods for methane emissions from manure management, emphasizing the need for more detailed and responsive inventory methodologies (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035008).
• CGIAR reviewed the state of agricultural climate change mitigation globally, focusing on the feasibility, opportunities, and challenges for achieving mitigation among smallholder farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/16715).
Canada.water: • CGIAR addressed water scarcity through community-based adaptation processes and Climate-Smart Agriculture options in Taung Khamauk Village, Myanmar (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123689).

Israel.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in Israel through adaptation and water management innovations. Specifically:
Israel.adaptation:
• CGIAR investigated adaptation measures to mitigate climate change impacts on agricultural production, emphasizing planned strategies like developing new crop varieties and improving irrigation technology (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR facilitated knowledge transfer and international collaboration for climate-resilient agronomy systems through the CLIFF-GRADS Science Collaboration Series (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096).
Israel.water:
• CGIAR qualified the LATEBLIGHT model for simulating Phytophthora infestans on potato foliage, demonstrating its sensitivity to weather variables in Israel (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/D8ZV2N).
• CGIAR extended the domain of applicability of the LATEBLIGHT model to include conditions observed in Israel, validating its predictions for potato cultivars (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/GTV7Y4).
• CGIAR assessed the performance of the LATEBLIGHT model in Nir-Eliyahu, Israel, confirming its sensitivity to weather variables (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/UZVDW6).
• CGIAR validated the LATEBLIGHT model for predicting late blight epidemics in Nir-Oz, Israel, highlighting its sensitivity to weather changes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XD4APA).
• CGIAR demonstrated the LATEBLIGHT model's applicability in Nir-Eliyahu, Israel, showing its sensitivity to weather and initial inoculum variables (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XIF8Q9).

Gambia.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and water management in Gambia through various strategies. Specifically:
Gambia.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in West Africa, including Gambia, to improve resilience of farming systems (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa, including Gambia, using adapted improved maize varieties (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed trade-offs and synergies of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, including Gambia, focusing on risk management and water management (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR used protection motivation theory to describe farmers’ adoption of climate change adaptation measures in the Central River Region of Gambia, highlighting the need for subsidized adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli7010013).
• CGIAR assessed the potential of climate-resilient crop varieties, crop diversification, and weather indexed-based insurance to build resilience to climate change in Gambia (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2022.834867).
• CGIAR explored farmers’ perception of climate variability and farm-level adaptation options in the Central River Region of Gambia, promoting drought-tolerant crops and water conservation techniques (https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos10070423).
Gambia.water:
• CGIAR analyzed two adaptation scenarios for sub-Saharan Africa, including Gambia, focusing on doubling the irrigated area and increasing crop yields to cope with climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR simulated scenarios for future food demand in Gambia, considering the impacts of climate change on crops and the adoption of sustainable irrigation infrastructure (https://doi.org/10.1007/s12571-024-01444-1).
• CGIAR collected household survey data on integrated land and water management for adaptation to climate variability and change in West Africa, including Gambia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• CGIAR proposed a conceptual framework to integrate climate change policies with agri-food systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138815).

Equatorial Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies in Equatorial Guinea through targeted research. Specifically:
- CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for agriculture sectors (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
- CGIAR examined biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Equatorial Guinea.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and policy developments for agriculture sectors in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR analyzed trade-offs and synergies of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR assessed adaptation scenarios to cope with climate change in Sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR mapped vulnerability of priority tree species to guide conservation and restoration efforts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
Equatorial Guinea.water: • CGIAR analyzed adaptation scenarios involving water resources and irrigation expansion (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

Sierra Leone.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in Sierra Leone through targeted research and adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the vulnerability of cocoa production to climate change and proposed adaptation strategies (Source_1). 
- CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors (Source_2).
Sierra Leone.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the vulnerability of cocoa to climate change and recommended adaptation strategies such as the use of shade trees to mitigate high temperatures (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2016.03.024).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in West Africa, including Sierra Leone (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined the implications of COVID-19 lockdowns during agricultural planting seasons on food security in Sub-Saharan Africa, including Sierra Leone (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41538-020-00073-0).
• CGIAR studied the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa, including Sierra Leone (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, including Sierra Leone (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR discussed a regional approach to climate change adaptation for cocoa in West Africa, including Sierra Leone (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y).
• CGIAR assessed heat stress conditions for dairy cattle production in West Africa under current and future climatic conditions, including Sierra Leone (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-020-02733-2).
• CGIAR collected household survey data on integrated land and water management for adaptation to climate variability and change in West Africa, including Sierra Leone (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
Sierra Leone.mitigation: • CGIAR assessed the role of trees on agricultural land and their significance for carbon sequestration, noting that Sierra Leone had one of the largest decreases in biomass carbon (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
Sierra Leone.water: • CGIAR collected household survey data on integrated land and water management for adaptation to climate variability and change in West Africa, including Sierra Leone (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

Chad.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies in Chad through various research initiatives. Specifically:
Chad.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for livestock, fishery, and crop production sectors in West Africa, including Chad, to improve resilience and promote climate-smart agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa, including Chad, using adapted improved maize varieties and bioeconomic models (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, including Chad, focusing on trade-offs and synergies related to decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR analyzed two adaptation scenarios for Sub-Saharan Africa, including Chad, to cope with climate change, using models that consider water-agriculture linkages and general equilibrium approaches (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR mapped the vulnerability of 100 priority tree species in Central Africa, including Chad, to guide conservation and restoration efforts against climate change and other anthropogenic threats (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
• CGIAR developed a country profile for Chad to initiate discussions on entry points for investing in climate-smart agriculture at scale, focusing on productivity, adaptation, and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114912).
• CGIAR explored the alignment of social protection and climate change adaptation objectives in the Sahel region, including Chad, to enhance adaptive capacities and climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/135122).
• CGIAR assessed the climate-smartness of the West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP) in Chad to strengthen the capacity of stakeholders to mainstream and implement climate-smart agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).
• CGIAR integrated and enhanced datasets on food security and household coping strategies in Chad, including data on climate hazards and extreme weather events (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SQA143).
• CGIAR developed the Conflict–Climate Pressure Index (CCPI) to enhance anticipatory capacity for food crises in the Sahel, including Chad, by integrating climate and conflict risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179392).
Chad.mitigation: • CGIAR highlighted the potential of climate-smart agriculture initiatives in Chad to reduce or remove greenhouse gases (GHGs) while enhancing resilience and productivity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114912).
Chad.water: • CGIAR analyzed adaptation scenarios for Sub-Saharan Africa, including Chad, that involved doubling the irrigated area and increasing crop yields to cope with climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

Bhutan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and mitigation in Bhutan through collaborative research and climate-smart agriculture initiatives. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on key crops and identified suitable locations for diversification crops (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80918). 
- CGIAR implemented climate-resilient agricultural value chains and integrated farming bodies (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
Bhutan.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of current and future heat stress on maize and the benefit of heat-tolerant varieties in South Asia, including Bhutan (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
• CGIAR analyzed the role of agriculture in nationally determined contributions to the Paris Agreement, focusing on adaptation initiatives and climate-resilient agricultural value chains (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• CGIAR described the emergence of networks like the Direct Seeded Rice Consortium (DSRC) to facilitate trans-disciplinary responses to climate change in rice-based systems in Asia, including Bhutan (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli8020035).
• CGIAR evaluated the role of evolving hybrid forms of adaptive knowledge for coping with environmental and social change in the Asian Highlands, including Bhutan (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on the climatic suitability of key crops in Bhutan and identified areas requiring interventions due to imminent loss of climate suitability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80918).
• CGIAR examined the suitability of key crops in Bhutan under current conditions and under a high emission climate scenario for the year 2050 (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU).
• CGIAR prioritized climate-smart agriculture technologies in South Asian countries, including Bhutan, to combat the impacts of climate change on agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/155261).
Bhutan.mitigation:
• CGIAR analyzed the role of agriculture in nationally determined contributions to the Paris Agreement, focusing on mitigation efforts and emission reduction commitments (https://doi.org/10.1080/14693062.2018.1501329).
• CGIAR highlighted the need for climate-smart agriculture initiatives that sustainably increase productivity, enhance resilience, and reduce/remove greenhouse gases (GHGs) in Bhutan (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/83338).
• CGIAR discussed the cooperative approach required to achieve an optimal solution to climate change, including the role of climate-vulnerable nations like Bhutan (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175781).

Netherlands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and greenhouse gas monitoring in the Netherlands through innovative research and policy frameworks. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed standardized methods for CO2 and energy flux monitoring (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).
- CGIAR assessed the suitability of protein-rich crops under future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
Netherlands.adaptation:
• CGIAR investigated adaptation measures to mitigate negative effects of climate change on agricultural production, focusing on research and development for new crop varieties and irrigation technology (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR enhanced the capacity of local communities to access crop genetic diversity for climate change adaptation, particularly in maize (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96090).
• CGIAR organized workshops to improve farm-household models for climate change adaptation, risk management, and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/24766).
• CGIAR designed a prototype tool to accelerate SME’s investment readiness in climate-smart agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114725).
• CGIAR supported climate services to enhance resilience of agricultural communities through participatory integrated climate services (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92851).
• CGIAR catalyzed sustainable finance co-investment to support resilient agribusiness through climate-informed business cases (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121858).
Netherlands.mitigation:
• CGIAR standardized processing methods for CO2 and energy flux monitoring to improve comparability and reliability of greenhouse gas observations (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).
• CGIAR explored low emission development options in large-scale dairy farms to identify and evaluate feasible mitigation options (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194).

Botswana.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and water management in Botswana through integrated approaches. Specifically:
Botswana.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation through the water-energy-food nexus, highlighting impacts on water, energy, and food resources in southern Africa and recommending strategies to build resilient communities (https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph15102306).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security in sub-Saharan Africa, using adapted improved maize varieties and bioeconomic models (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR assessed gender-based differences in access, use, and benefits from rural climate services, identifying pathways for making climate services more responsive to the needs of rural women and men (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review of climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, identifying potential trade-offs and synergies related to decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR analyzed two possible adaptation scenarios to climate change for Sub-Saharan Africa, focusing on increasing irrigated area and crop yields (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR examined the impact of low-frequency, high-impact weather events on crop yields in southern Africa, projecting significant yield declines and increased food insecurity (https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2022.787582).
• CGIAR reviewed migration as an adaptation strategy due to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes, examining the evidence on human mobility as a response to climate hazards (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
• CGIAR promoted Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) as a means to incorporate climate change adaptation and mitigation into agricultural development planning and investment strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34813).
• CGIAR examined the food security threats facing southern Africa and explored how climate change will increase the efforts needed to achieve sustainable food security (https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896292086).
Botswana.mitigation: • CGIAR promoted Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) as a means to incorporate climate change mitigation into agricultural development planning and investment strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34813).
Botswana.water:
• CGIAR investigated the coupled human and natural system linking climate, sanitation, and groundwater quality in Ramotswa, Botswana, highlighting critical indirect linkages between climate change, sanitation, groundwater quality, and water security (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10040-018-1901-4).
• CGIAR analyzed two possible adaptation scenarios to climate change for Sub-Saharan Africa, focusing on increasing irrigated area and crop yields, with a general equilibrium model including water resources (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).

Panama.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation strategies in Panama through agroforestry, climate-smart agriculture, and policy frameworks. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the suitability of cocoa and agroforestry systems under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7). 
- CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and pollinators under future climates (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114). 
- CGIAR developed climate-smart food systems business models for scaling resilient practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
Panama.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the vulnerability of cocoa and tree species to future climate conditions and proposed agroforestry system transformations (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-45491-7).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and pollinators under future climates to understand coupled impacts of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR developed a framework for climate-smart agriculture and adaptation measures through Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114943).
• CGIAR evaluated the role of biological corridors for facilitating plant dispersal under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672).
• CGIAR assessed the suitability of cocoa and agroforestry systems under climate change using Species Distribution Modelling (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1).
• CGIAR implemented dialogue spaces to understand climate behavior and generate recommendations to reduce risks associated with climate variability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108780).
Panama.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched the mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests and adaptations to climate change in agriculture (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).
• CGIAR developed climate-smart food systems business models for scaling climate mitigation and resilient practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116921).
• CGIAR accounted for silvopastoral systems’ contributions to national GHG inventories and their mitigation potential (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111256).

France.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and adaptation strategies in France through innovative research and policy assessments. Specifically:
- CGIAR explored heat stress impacts on maize yields and adaptation strategies (Source 1).
- CGIAR analyzed protein-rich crop suitability under future climates (Source 3).
France.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the increasing influence of heat stress on French maize yields and the need for improved technology to maintain yield levels (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12069).
• CGIAR analyzed the heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, including France (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• CGIAR investigated the perceptions of European forest owners and managers regarding the active adaptation management of forest genetic resources (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• CGIAR studied historical reconfigurations of a social–ecological system adapting to economic, policy, and climate changes in the French Alps (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-021-01760-8).
• CGIAR reviewed G20 countries' recommendations, including France, for healthy and sustainable food system policies to build climate resilience (https://doi.org/10.4060/cc2805en).
• CGIAR modeled adaptation measures in agriculture, including new crop varieties and irrigation technology (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR reviewed Green Growth policy actions related to agriculture and climate change, including France (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33278).
France.mitigation:
• CGIAR introduced standardized processing methods for CO2 and energy fluxes calculation at ICOS ecosystem stations, including in France (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).

Dominican Republic.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in the Dominican Republic through targeted adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed climate-smart cocoa practices to address risks from climate change (Source_7). 
- CGIAR assessed climate exposure maps for site-specific Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) recommendations (Source_12).
Dominican Republic.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed climate-smart cocoa practices to address risks from climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79873).
• CGIAR implemented climate analysis to support efficient adaptation at scale and improve resilience at farm level (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103775).
• CGIAR strengthened capacities for the scaling of Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees to enhance climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174584).
• CGIAR identified appropriate Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices aligned to specific risk types and climate exposure maps (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123193).
• CGIAR modeled the potential distributions of coffee and coffee pollinators under current and future climates to understand future coffee-suitable areas (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).

Solomon Islands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation strategies in Solomon Islands through various innovative approaches. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed the economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies for the fisheries sector, highlighting the potential decline in per capita fish consumption without adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004).
- CGIAR highlighted the resilience and unique territorial management techniques of indigenous food systems in Solomon Islands, emphasizing the need for systemized research on these systems (https://doi.org/10.4060/cb5131en).
- CGIAR analyzed the economic impact of climate change and adaptation strategies in the fisheries sector, projecting a significant increase in fish demand and a slow growth in domestic fish production due to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).
- CGIAR discussed the climate vulnerability of coastal and marine biodiversity in Solomon Islands, emphasizing the need for national policies for environmental conservation and preservation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022).
- CGIAR examined migration as an adaptation strategy due to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes, highlighting the mixed outcomes of migration for vulnerable households (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
- CGIAR provided insights into the impacts of climate change on Pacific Island food systems, including fisheries and forests, and offered essential information for policy-makers to enhance resilience and strengthen adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).
- CGIAR analyzed the limited capacity for adapting to climate change in Solomon Islands, highlighting the vulnerability of the country's agriculture and fisheries sectors (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066).
- CGIAR developed community-based resource management solutions tailored to local environmental features and fishing characteristics in Solomon Islands (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SFZNWM).
- CGIAR assessed adaptation options for climate change through participatory action research, supporting community-level decision-making for adaptation in Solomon Islands (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495).

CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation in Papua New Guinea through gender-responsive services, agricultural resilience, and food system analysis. Specifically:

Afghanistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and water resource management in Afghanistan through innovative research and adaptive strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed the impact of heat stress on maize yields and the benefits of heat-tolerant varieties (Source 1). 
- CGIAR coupled remote sensing and hydrological modeling to evaluate climate change impacts on streamflow in the Kabul River Basin (Source 2).
Afghanistan.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of heat stress on maize yields and the potential benefits of heat-tolerant varieties to minimize yield loss due to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
• CGIAR evaluated the impacts of climate extremes in the Kabul River Basin and emphasized the need for prompt climate actions to increase adaptive capacity and build resilient livelihoods (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10113-023-02167-3).
• CGIAR integrated local hybrid knowledge and state support for climate change adaptation in the Asian Highlands, highlighting the importance of targeted state support for locally evolving hybrid knowledge (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).
• CGIAR examined migration as an adaptation strategy due to freshwater and inland hydroclimatic changes, discussing the multifaceted outcomes of migration and the need for informed climate and migration policies (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-023-03573-6).
• CGIAR advanced climate services in South Asia, focusing on strengthening climate resilience through co-producing weather and climate services and enhancing coordination across the region (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cliser.2022.100295).
• CGIAR conducted a study on the phenotypic data for the Genome-Wide Association Study (GWAS) of the International Treaty Project Wheat Exchange Set, aiming to improve wheat production and its resilience to climate change through maintaining the diversity of currently grown landraces (hdl:11529/10548355).
Afghanistan.mitigation: • CGIAR examined the climate vulnerabilities of Central Asia's water, agriculture, and energy sectors, highlighting the need for international development finance to strengthen climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Afghanistan.water:
• CGIAR coupled remote sensing and hydrological modeling to evaluate the impacts of climate change on streamflow in the Kabul River Basin, providing a methodology for sustainable management of water resources (https://doi.org/10.3390/su132414025).
• CGIAR assessed the performance of the Soil and Water Assessment Tool (SWAT) model by statistical indicators and evaluated streamflow under changing climate scenarios in the Kabul River Basin (https://doi.org/10.3390/w14111697).
• CGIAR summarized changing climate trends in three Afghan–Pakistani transboundary river basins, highlighting the consistent rise in temperature and the uncertainty associated with precipitation (https://doi.org/10.1079/9781800622371.0004).

Finland.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and food systems sustainability in Finland through targeted research and adaptation strategies. Specifically:

Finland.adaptation:

• Researchers emphasized resource management and sustainability in agriculture to address climate change impacts on water (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154089).

CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and mitigation strategies in Uruguay through innovative research and policy frameworks. Specifically:

Denmark.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture in Denmark through innovative modeling and adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to evaluate responses to climate change (Source 1).
- CGIAR investigated adaptation measures to mitigate negative effects of climate change on agricultural production (Source 7).
Denmark.adaptation:
• CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to quantify variation among models and evaluate responses to climate change, including temperature sensitivity and yield responses to climate variability (Source 1).
• CGIAR analyzed the heterogeneous impacts of climate change on crop suitability, emphasizing the need for breeding and research planning strategies to improve the tolerance of protein-rich crops to abiotic stresses (Source 4).
• CGIAR surveyed forest owners and managers to understand their perceptions of threats to forest ecosystems and their attitudes toward actively managing forest genetic resources to strengthen resilience to climate change (Source 5).
• CGIAR investigated adaptation measures to mitigate negative effects of climate change on agricultural production, highlighting strategies for reducing risk and improving resilience (Source 7).
• CGIAR evaluated agronomic traits on reference set for fodder quality in Sorghum under drought stress and well-irrigated conditions (Source 8).
• CGIAR conducted genetic dissection and QTL mapping of fodder quality traits in Sorghum under different water regimes to identify quantitative trait loci associated with fodder quality (Source 10).
Denmark.mitigation:
• CGIAR introduced the steps of the processing chain applied to the eddy covariance data of ICOS stations for the estimation of final CO2, water, and energy fluxes, ensuring standardised processing for all stations (Source 2).
• CGIAR highlighted alternative agricultural practices tailored to different regions that show promise for reducing net GHG emissions and maintaining or improving yields despite extreme weather (Source 3).
Denmark.water:
• CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to evaluate responses to climate change, including evapotranspiration (ET), water use efficiency, and rainfall impacts on nonirrigated sites (Source 1).
• CGIAR evaluated agronomic traits on reference set for fodder quality in Sorghum under drought stress and well-irrigated conditions (Source 8).
• CGIAR conducted genetic dissection and QTL mapping of fodder quality traits in Sorghum under different water regimes to identify quantitative trait loci associated with fodder quality (Source 10).

Belize.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation and mitigation in Belize through targeted research and policy development. Specifically: 
- CGIAR modeled climate change impacts on smallholder agriculture and ecosystems (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed coupled effects of climate change on coffee suitability and pollinators (Source 2).
Belize.adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled climate change impacts on smallholder agriculture and ecosystems, emphasizing the need for national and local adaptation responses (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1867-y).
• CGIAR coupled pollination services and coffee suitability under climate change, suggesting management practices like forest conservation and shade adjustment (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1617940114).
• CGIAR formulated the Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) strategy for the Central American Integration System (SICA), involving participatory processes and policy development (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89328).
• CGIAR researched climate change policies in Latin America, focusing on adaptations to climate change in agriculture (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).
• CGIAR developed a multi-criteria framework for identifying and prioritizing CSA practices and technologies in Belize (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100495).
• CGIAR advanced the implementation of the CSA regional strategy for 2018-2030, using future scenarios methodology (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429).
• CGIAR shared information on expected climate behavior and impacts, providing recommendations for the agricultural sector, particularly in the context of COVID-19 (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111468, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111467).
Belize.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched the mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions from forests and adaptations to climate change in agriculture (https://doi.org/10.3390/su9101831).

Austria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and food systems in Austria through targeted research and policy engagement. Specifically: 
- CGIAR analyzed protein-rich crop suitability under climate change (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed forest genetic resource management for climate adaptation (Source 2).
Austria.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the impact of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, highlighting regional differences and the need for adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• CGIAR investigated forest owners' and managers' perceptions of climate change threats and their attitudes toward active adaptation management of forest genetic resources (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• CGIAR modeled adaptation measures in agriculture, proposing strategies like developing new crop varieties and improving irrigation technology to mitigate climate change impacts (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR studied the impact of climate change on electricity generation and demand patterns in Europe, focusing on hydropower and resilience to extreme weather events (https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862).
Austria.mitigation: • CGIAR, along with partners, proposed food systems as a key topic for the Sharm el-Sheikh mitigation ambition and implementation work programme (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087).
Austria.water: • CGIAR examined the impact of climate change on hydropower generation, particularly hydro run-of-river (RoR) systems, and its implications for electricity demand and supply in Europe (https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-egu23-13862).

Spain.narrative: CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient agriculture and forestry in Spain through innovative adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically:
Spain.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the suitability of protein-rich crops under current and future European climates, highlighting the need for breeding strategies to improve tolerance to abiotic stresses in faba bean, lentil, and chickpea (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• CGIAR surveyed forest owners and managers across Europe to understand their perceptions of threats to forest ecosystems and their attitudes toward managing forest genetic resources to strengthen resilience to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10342-020-01311-6).
• CGIAR explored how traditional agroecological knowledge can transform conventional agri-food systems in Spain, emphasizing the importance of scaling deep, out, and up to enable broader transformation (https://doi.org/10.3390/su11051222).
• CGIAR investigated adaptation measures in agriculture, including research and development for new crop varieties and changes in irrigation technology to mitigate negative effects of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1787/5jxrclljnbxq-en).
• CGIAR reported on the adaptive responses of trees to biotic or abiotic selection pressures, cataloging candidate genes for phenological and drought-related traits in important tree families (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11295-011-0378-2).
• CGIAR documented efforts by pastoralist women in Spain to restore rangelands, improving livelihoods and benefiting the environment (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128162).
Spain.mitigation: • CGIAR standardized the processing of eddy covariance data for CO2 and energy fluxes at ICOS ecosystem stations, ensuring reliable and comparable results for greenhouse gas monitoring (https://doi.org/10.1515/intag-2017-0043).
Spain.water: • CGIAR conducted a water stress experiment in a Mediterranean area (Barrax, Spain), using active in situ and passive airborne fluorescence measurements to detect water stress on a fescue field (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/7QY6KU).

Djibouti.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience and food security in Djibouti through targeted research and adaptation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR examined climate change impacts on maize production and food security (Source 1). 
- CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers (Source 2).
Djibouti.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined climate change impacts on maize production and food security, including yield reductions and adaptation measures for future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers, focusing on trade-offs and synergies in decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR analyzed adaptation scenarios to cope with climate change, including increasing irrigated area and crop yields in Sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR provided a unified resource on agricultural production systems in relation to food and nutrition security and climate change, focusing on livestock systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995).
• CGIAR advanced the use of climate information for risk management in the Horn of Africa, including agro-climatic variables for the agriculture and food security sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92093).
• CGIAR built capacities in objective seasonal forecasting and strengthened institutional linkages for climate services in the agriculture and food security sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941).
• CGIAR supported climate change adaptation in livestock and pastoralism through research-for-development partnerships (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053).
• CGIAR developed a Climate Action Plan for the East and Horn of Africa and Great Lakes Region, outlining climate adaptation measures (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370).
Djibouti.mitigation:
• CGIAR reviewed greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and climate-smart agriculture (CSA) in relation to agricultural production systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106995).
• CGIAR conducted capacity-building trainings on the attribution of climate change and its impacts for African climate negotiators (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
Djibouti.water:
• CGIAR analyzed economy-wide impacts of climate change on agriculture, including water resources and adaptation scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR advanced the use of climate information for risk management, including water management strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92093).

Malta.adaptation: • CGIAR assessed the heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Malta, emphasizing the need for breeding strategies to improve crop resilience to abiotic stresses (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).

Sao Tome And Principe.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation and water management in Sao Tome And Principe through targeted research and modeling. Specifically: 
- CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security (Source_1). 
- CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (Source_2). 
- CGIAR analyzed economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture (Source_3). 
- CGIAR mapped vulnerability of priority tree species to guide conservation and restoration efforts (Source_4).
Sao Tome And Principe.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined the biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production and food security, including yield reductions and adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers, focusing on trade-offs and synergies in decision-making and implementation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.regsus.2021.05.002).
• CGIAR analyzed two adaptation scenarios to cope with climate change, including doubling irrigated area and increasing crop yields (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR mapped the vulnerability of 100 priority tree species to climate change and other anthropogenic threats to guide conservation and restoration efforts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biocon.2022.109554).
Sao Tome And Principe.water:
• CGIAR analyzed economywide impacts of climate change on agriculture, including water resources and adaptation scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2013.05.006).
• CGIAR examined the biophysical impacts of climate change on maize production, including water management and yield reductions (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Turkmenistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate resilience in Turkmenistan through interdisciplinary research. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climatic risks to livestock production (Source 1).
- CGIAR examined water scarcity and adaptation strategies (Source 2).
- CGIAR analyzed climate vulnerabilities in water, agriculture, and energy sectors (Source 3).
Turkmenistan.adaptation:
• CGIAR identified climatic risks to livestock production, including declines in vegetation biomass and increased variability in grazing availability (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR advocated for innovative research pathways to scrutinize smallholder adaptation strategies and examine the nexus between climate change, water scarcity, and mobility (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).
• CGIAR quantified exposure, sensitivities, and adaptive capacities of Central Asia's water, agriculture, and energy sectors, emphasizing the need for international development finance to strengthen climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Turkmenistan.mitigation: • CGIAR examined the global imperative to achieve carbon neutrality by the middle of the century, highlighting the economic burden on lower-income provinces (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Turkmenistan.water:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on water resources, including projections of river discharge under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 scenarios (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR analyzed water scarcity due to increasing water demand and the effects of climate change, such as rising temperatures and loss of glacier mass (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2024.2436090).

Oman.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized agricultural research and development infrastructure and forage cultivation practices in Oman. Specifically:
- CGIAR collected and analyzed data on financial, human, and institutional resources for agricultural R&D (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IR12XU).
- CGIAR established Farmers Field Schools to demonstrate forage cultivation practices for livestock feed (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/36076).

Nauru.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate mitigation in Nauru through policy advocacy. Specifically: 
- CGIAR contributed to a prescriptive agenda for low-carbon transformation and GHG mitigation in response to the Republic of Nauru's call for action (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68171).
Nauru.mitigation: • CGIAR supported the Republic of Nauru in advocating for a global low-carbon transformation agenda, emphasizing the need for decisive political action to scale up cost-effective technologies and reduce emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68171).

Georgia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate vulnerabilities and adaptive capacities in Central Asia, including Georgia, through comprehensive sectoral assessments. Specifically:
- CGIAR examined climate vulnerabilities of water, agriculture, and energy sectors at the province level (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
- CGIAR assessed adaptive capacities and economic burdens of climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Georgia.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed adaptive capacities and climate resilience in Central Asia's provinces, including Georgia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR highlighted the need for international development finance to strengthen climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Georgia.mitigation: • CGIAR researched GHG mitigation and carbon neutrality goals in Central Asia's energy sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
Georgia.water:
• CGIAR analyzed climate vulnerabilities of water resources in Central Asia, including river discharge projections under RCP 2.6 and RCP 8.5 scenarios (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).
• CGIAR identified varying trends in water resources across provinces, with potential declines in southern river basins (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128220).

general / cross-cutting.narrative: CGIAR prioritized cross-cutting research in climate change adaptation, mitigation, and water resource management through comprehensive assessments and innovative methodologies.
general / cross-cutting.adaptation:
• CGIAR synthesized the state of knowledge on climate change adaptation, recognizing the interdependence of climate, ecosystems, and human societies, and identifying resilient development pathways (https://doi.org/10.59327/ipcc/ar6-9789291691647).
• CGIAR evaluated the ability of climate envelope models to predict species distributions under different climates, providing insights into future climate projections and past climate reconstructions (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-2486.2006.01256.x).
general / cross-cutting.mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of land-use change and management on soil organic carbon, highlighting the role of biochar and organic amendments in climate regulation (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-39338-z).
• CGIAR analyzed the environmental impacts of meeting global food demand through sustainable intensification of agriculture, focusing on greenhouse gas emissions and nitrogen use (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1116437108).
• CGIAR provided ecosystem-scale data on CO2 exchange through the FLUXNET2015 dataset, supporting ecophysiology studies and Earth system models (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0534-3).
• CGIAR examined the planetary boundaries framework, emphasizing the importance of climate change mitigation within the Earth System (https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1259855).
• CGIAR reviewed the contributions of food systems to greenhouse gas emissions and the potential for reductions in waste to mitigate climate change (https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-environ-020411-130608).
general / cross-cutting.water:
• CGIAR projected the impacts of climate change on water resources, highlighting the potential for severe decreases in water availability and increased water scarcity (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222460110).
• CGIAR analyzed the effects of human interventions and climate change on the terrestrial water cycle, identifying areas of irrigation water scarcity (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1222475110).

rice.narrative: CGIAR prioritized rice research through adaptive management, climate impact assessments, and water management strategies. Specifically:
rice.adaptation:
• CGIAR integrated models of farmers’ decision-making with biophysical crop modeling to simulate adaptation of crop calendars and its effect on rice yields under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-022-34411-5).
• CGIAR developed a global dataset for projected impacts of climate change on rice yields, with and without adaptation measures (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-022-01150-7).
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on rice production in Africa, identifying adaptation options such as shifting sowing dates and using varieties with a high temperature sum (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13967).
• CGIAR evaluated the vulnerability of rice production to climate change, particularly in regions like the Mekong Delta, and identified adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0213641).
• CGIAR studied the effects of climate factors on rice quality, providing information for new breeding strategies to develop varieties adapted to a changing world (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0066218).
rice.mitigation:
• CGIAR researched greenhouse gas emissions from rice fields in Bangladesh, comparing emissions from alternate wetting and drying (AWD) irrigation and continuous flooding (CF) (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.139382).
• CGIAR calculated the greenhouse gas emissions associated with rice production in India, identifying mitigation options to reduce emissions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2016.12.024).
rice.water:
• CGIAR simulated impacts on rice in irrigated and rainfed systems, identifying water availability constraints and the need for improved water management (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13967).
• CGIAR quantified the physical area of multiple cropping systems, including rice, and the potential for increasing cropping intensity (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2020.102131).
• CGIAR assessed the impacts of El Niño Southern Oscillation on the global yields of rice, highlighting the importance of seasonal temperature and precipitation (https://doi.org/10.1038/ncomms4712).

livestock (general/beef).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized livestock adaptation and mitigation strategies across various regions. Specifically: 
- CGIAR analyzed adaptation measures for livestock systems (Source_1). 
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on livestock supply chains (Source_2).
livestock (general/beef).adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the perception and adaptation to climate change by farmers in the Nile basin of Ethiopia, highlighting factors like livestock ownership and extension services (https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021859610000687).
• CGIAR reviewed the risk of climate-related impacts along the land-based livestock food supply chain, emphasizing the need for adaptation in hot, resource-limited environments (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2020.100488).
• CGIAR examined climate change impacts on livestock and livestock systems in developing countries, identifying key researchable issues for smallholders and pastoralists (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2009.05.002).
• CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on global rangeland ecosystem services, projecting declines in livestock and economic losses (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13995).
• CGIAR studied land-use and land-cover dynamics in southwestern Ethiopia, linking changes to drought, migration, and livestock disease (https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1008177712995).
• CGIAR identified climatic risks to global rangeland production systems, projecting declines in biomass and increases in variability (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ab7395).
• CGIAR analyzed the vulnerability of households dependent on livestock and the capacity of these systems to adapt (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9210-9).
livestock (general/beef).mitigation:
• CGIAR projected emissions reductions through livestock system transitions and climate mitigation policies (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1308044111).
• CGIAR emphasized the importance of accurate estimates of livestock greenhouse gas emissions for effective mitigation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anifeedsci.2011.04.083).
• CGIAR explored mitigation potentials of technologies and management practices for enteric methane emissions in Latin America (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.00065).
• CGIAR assessed the carbon sequestration potential and mitigation options for the livestock sector (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-009-9210-9).

agroforestry.narrative: CGIAR prioritized agroforestry research across multiple climate change dimensions. Specifically:
agroforestry.adaptation:
• CGIAR reviewed the role of forest genetic resources in adapting to climate change, emphasizing management strategies to conserve evolutionary potential (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foreco.2014.04.006).
• CGIAR analyzed how agroforestry alters microclimates, hydrology, and biogeochemistry in Sub-Saharan Africa, linking it to human health outcomes (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oneear.2019.10.017).
• CGIAR advocated for climate-smart reforestation to address both adaptation and mitigation objectives, ensuring resilience to altered hydrological cycles (https://doi.org/10.1111/rec.12209).
• CGIAR examined the roles of trees and agroforestry in enhancing smallholder resilience to climate-related risks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.013).
agroforestry.mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the global contribution of agroforestry to carbon sequestration, highlighting its role in climate change mitigation (https://doi.org/10.1038/srep29987).
• CGIAR conducted a spatial analysis of global land suitability for Clean Development Mechanism afforestation and reforestation projects (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2008.01.014).
• CGIAR identified the most effective agroforestry systems for soil and above-ground carbon sequestration in different world regions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2017.11.032).
• CGIAR discussed the importance of measuring and reporting agroforestry in UNFCCC national communications for carbon sequestration (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2019.106569).
• CGIAR analyzed the governance challenges in REDD+ projects aimed at reducing deforestation and forest degradation (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2018.02.015).
agroforestry.water:
• CGIAR reviewed climate-smart agriculture technologies, including agroforestry, for soil and water conservation in West Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2018.03.199).
• CGIAR highlighted the role of tropical reforestation in addressing altered hydrological cycles (https://doi.org/10.1111/rec.12209).

maize.narrative: CGIAR prioritized maize research across multiple climate ontology categories, focusing on adaptation strategies, water-related impacts, and mitigation approaches.
maize.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the potential impacts of climate change on maize production in Africa and Latin America, highlighting the need for household-level assessments to target vulnerable populations for poverty alleviation (https://doi.org/10.1016/s0959-3780(02)00090-0).
• CGIAR quantified the impact of climate change on maize yield and the potential benefits of incorporating drought and heat tolerance into commonly grown maize varieties (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2017.10.001).
• CGIAR assessed the potential climate change impact on maize and the potential of cultivar adaptation to climate change for rainfed cereals in sub-Saharan Africa (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2024.127137).
• CGIAR reviewed achievements in stress tolerance breeding and presented future tools for quick and efficient germplasm development for maize (https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7).
• CGIAR examined smallholder farmers’ perceptions of climate change and their adaptation strategies in southwestern Nigeria (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001).
• CGIAR evaluated the response of maize to a changing climate in East Africa, emphasizing the importance of localized, community-based efforts to increase local adaptive capacity (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2008.08.005).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of current and future heat stress on maize and the benefit of heat-tolerant varieties in South Asia (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00704-016-1931-6).
maize.mitigation: • CGIAR discussed sustainable agronomic and resource management practices that can effectively contribute to climate change mitigation in maize production (https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-394275-3.00006-7).
maize.water:
• CGIAR quantified how modes of climate variability contribute to maize production variance, highlighting the importance of monitoring and predicting simultaneous crop failures (https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.aaw1976).
• CGIAR assessed the likelihood of investment in various Climate Smart Agriculture technology combinations, including soil and water conservation management practices (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2019.05.013).
• CGIAR presented an explicit rationale and methodology for selecting data sources for simulating crop yields and estimating yield gaps, which is relevant for simulating the impact of climate change and land-use change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2015.03.004).
• CGIAR compared smallholder farmers’ perception of climate change with meteorological data, highlighting the reduction in rainfall and its impact on maize production (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2016.12.001).

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized wheat research through climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed multi-model ensembles to simulate global wheat yield and quality under climate change (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed the impacts of climate change on wheat production and adaptation strategies (Source 2).
wheat.adaptation:
• CGIAR tested and applied a 32-multi-model ensemble to simulate global wheat yield and quality under climate change, highlighting the need for genotypes adapted to warmer temperatures (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14481).
• CGIAR simulated climate change impacts and adaptation strategies for wheat globally using new crop genetic traits (CGT), including increased heat tolerance and late flowering (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/abd970).
• CGIAR used crowdsourced citizen science to characterize varietal climatic responses and improve variety recommendations for climate adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1813720116).
• CGIAR coupled a wheat crop simulation model with a wheat blast model to provide quantitative global estimates of wheat blast vulnerability under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-023-01902-2).
• CGIAR presented ensemble projections of crop productivity that account for biophysical processes, inherent uncertainty, and adaptation, using spring wheat in Northeast China as a case study (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/5/3/034012).
• CGIAR investigated the effects of integrating different climate-smart agricultural practices on crop production, soil fertility, and carbon sequestration (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515).
• CGIAR evaluated the impacts of the 2015 Paris Agreement range of global warming (1.5 and 2.0°C warming above the pre-industrial period) on global wheat production and local yield variability (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14542).
• CGIAR analyzed the seasonal spatiotemporal climate variability in the Borena zone of Ethiopia and its effects on agriculture and livestock production (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-08-2024-0133).
• CGIAR showed current elite nurseries express a range of levels of heat adaptation and the need to revisit breeding strategies to integrate genetic diversity that confers climate resilience (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41558-024-02069-0).
• CGIAR assessed the regional vulnerability of wheat production to climate change in India and projected the impacts and adaptation gains (https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212).
wheat.mitigation: • CGIAR demonstrated that climate-smart agricultural practices (CSA) can mitigate GHG emissions through soil carbon sequestration (https://doi.org/10.3390/su13084515).
wheat.water:
• CGIAR analyzed the effects of climate variability on agriculture and livestock production, particularly wheat and livestock production, in the Borena zone of Ethiopia (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-08-2024-0133).
• CGIAR assessed the vulnerability of wheat production to climate change in India and projected the impacts of simple adaptation options, such as change in sowing times and increased and efficient use of inputs (https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212).
