roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient potato production through integrated strategies. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed an integrated seed health strategy to mitigate seed degeneration in developing countries (Source 1).
- CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to evaluate responses to climate change (Source 2).
roots and tubers.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed an integrated seed health strategy to improve the health status of seed tubers in informal systems, integrating disease resistance and on-farm management tools (https://doi.org/10.1111/ppa.12439).
• CGIAR analyzed the adoption of potato varieties and their role for climate change adaptation in India, comparing state-level climate change projections with adoption of high resistant and tolerant potato varieties (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.01.001).
• CGIAR reviewed ecophysiological strategies to cope and adapt to changes in atmospheric CO2 levels, temperature, and soil water availability in the Andean region (https://doi.org/10.1515/opag-2018-0029).
• CGIAR emphasized the development and adaptation of Integrated Pest Management (IPM) across all potato-growing regions to address future pest risks under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-28683-5_8).
• CGIAR presented an analytical framework for effects mediated by plant disease, incorporating consideration of multiple biological interactions and environmental thresholds (https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1365-3059.2010.02409.x).
• CGIAR explored the potential of wild relatives of potato to contribute to adaptation to future climate scenarios through introgression of novel traits (https://doi.org/10.1002/fes3.360).
• CGIAR examined the global effect of climate change on potato late blight, considering three global climate models for the A2 greenhouse gas emission scenario (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.12587).
• CGIAR tested the effect of legume intercropping and water-soluble silicon on soil erosion and use efficiency of light and water in rain-fed potato systems (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345).
• CGIAR assessed farmers’ perception of and coping strategies to climate change in Uganda, focusing on drought tolerance and planting drought-tolerant varieties (https://doi.org/10.5539/jas.v5n8p252).
roots and tubers.water:
• CGIAR conducted a potato crop multimodel assessment to evaluate responses to climate change, focusing on evapotranspiration (ET), water use efficiency, and rainfall (W) at nonirrigated sites (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.13411).
• CGIAR tested the effect of legume intercropping and water-soluble silicon on soil erosion and use efficiency of light and water in rain-fed potato systems (https://doi.org/10.3389/fsufs.2020.566345).

legumes.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized legume adaptation to climate change through genetic improvement, policy planning, and gender-inclusive strategies. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed genetic improvement strategies for common bean using wild relatives and closely related species (Source_1). 
- CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe (Source_2).
legumes.adaptation:
• CGIAR used wild relatives and closely related species to adapt common bean to climate change through genetic improvement (https://doi.org/10.3390/agronomy3020433).
• CGIAR analyzed the heterogeneous impacts of climate change on protein-rich crop suitability in Europe, emphasizing the need for breeding strategies to improve tolerance to abiotic stresses (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eja.2019.125974).
• CGIAR presented an integrated research framework combining genomics, systems biology, physiology, modeling, and breeding for legume improvement under elevated CO2 conditions (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpb.2020.100149).
• CGIAR assessed gender disparities in farmers’ access and use of climate-smart agriculture technologies and practices in Southern Tanzania (https://doi.org/10.1007/s43621-025-01150-8).
• CGIAR designed weather index insurance for crops to increase farmer satisfaction and stabilize income by providing timely payouts linked with weather parameters (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.crm.2019.100189).
• CGIAR developed an interdisciplinary research agenda to improve tolerance of common bean to abiotic stresses through modern genotyping and breeding approaches (https://doi.org/10.1071/fp11102).
• CGIAR estimated yield changes in drybeans due to changing climate in Central America using the DSSAT model (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-015-9696-2).
• CGIAR improved spatial modeling of crop suitability using a new digital soil map of Tanzania, incorporating soil organic carbon content (https://doi.org/10.1080/02571862.2017.1281447).
• CGIAR determined the differences in the perception and choice of adaptation strategies between men and women farmers in Mali (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10668-021-01242-1).
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change adaptation on farm-level revenue among soybean farmers in Togo (https://doi.org/10.1080/23311932.2020.1743625).
legumes.water: • CGIAR improved spatial modeling of crop suitability by incorporating soil organic carbon content, which is critical for common bean production in Tanzania (https://doi.org/10.1080/02571862.2017.1281447).

coffee and cocoa.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate change adaptation strategies for coffee and cocoa production through innovative research and modeling. Specifically: 
- CGIAR projected climate suitability for coffee production and identified adaptation strategies (Source_1). 
- CGIAR analyzed vulnerability to climate change in West African cocoa and proposed adaptation measures (Source_2).
coffee and cocoa.adaptation:
• CGIAR projected climate suitability for coffee production, indicating higher temperatures may reduce yields and increasing variability of intra-seasonal temperatures could affect Coffea canephora, with a global reduction in suitable coffee areas by about 50% (Source_1).
• CGIAR analyzed vulnerability to climate change in West African cocoa, finding that maximum dry season temperatures will become more limiting than water availability, and proposed adaptation strategies like shade trees (Source_2).
• CGIAR developed a climate change adaptation strategy for coffee communities in Mexico, focusing on sustainable land use practices and forest conservation (Source_3).
• CGIAR created an adaptation framework for coffee production in Nicaragua, distinguishing between incremental and transformative adaptation strategies (Source_4).
• CGIAR modeled global shifts in Coffea arabica suitability due to climate change, predicting decreased areas suitable for production in Mesoamerica and shifts among major producing regions (Source_5).
• CGIAR demonstrated that increasing nighttime temperatures significantly reduce Coffea arabica yields in Tanzania, highlighting the need for adaptation strategies (Source_6).
• CGIAR emphasized the role of elevated atmospheric CO2 in mitigating harmful effects of supra-optimal temperatures on coffee physiology (Source_7).
• CGIAR presented a framework for prioritizing adaptation approaches for agriculture, including robust adaptation options for coffee in Latin America (Source_8).
• CGIAR developed an integrated framework for assessing vulnerability to climate change and identifying adaptation strategies for coffee growers in Mesoamerica (Source_9).
• CGIAR assessed the suitability of replacing coffee with cocoa and integrating trees in agroforestry systems to ameliorate abiotic stress in Mesoamerica (Source_10).
coffee and cocoa.mitigation: • CGIAR researched the role of elevated atmospheric CO2 in mitigating harmful effects of supra-optimal temperatures on coffee physiology (Source_7).

fisheries and aquaculture.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate adaptation strategies in fisheries and aquaculture through various innovations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR synthesized pathways through which climate variability impacts fisherfolk livelihoods (Source_1). 
- CGIAR assessed the economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies in Solomon Islands (Source_2).
fisheries and aquaculture.adaptation:
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate variability and change on fishery-based livelihoods, identifying adaptation strategies and exploring implications for local livelihoods, fisheries management, and climate policies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2009.08.007).
• CGIAR assessed the potential economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies, including natural resource management (NRM), fish aggregating devices (FAD), and aquaculture in Solomon Islands (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004).
• CGIAR evaluated the economic impact of climate change adaptation strategies, such as NRM and aquaculture, in Fiji (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.023).
• CGIAR estimated the economic costs of potential climate change adaptation strategies for the fisheries sector in Vanuatu and Timor-Leste (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.010).
• CGIAR developed a supply-and-demand model to assess the effect of climate change and related adaptation strategies in four Pacific coral triangle countries (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.011).
• CGIAR examined the socio-economic consequences of climate change on small-scale fish producers in Sub-Saharan Africa and proposed adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1002/aff2.182).
• CGIAR discussed the economic contributions, types of fisheries, and national development plans and policies related to the fisheries sector in Pacific Island countries (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2015.12.022).
• CGIAR studied climate change effects on ecological productivity, fishers’ incomes, and fish consumption for an artisanal open-access fishery and proposed policy interventions (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220433).
• CGIAR collected household data to understand the capabilities of recovering from the shock of the flood in Bangladesh (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UJA9N5).
• CGIAR provided insights into the different dimensions of the food system, including fisheries, to enhance resilience and strengthen adaptation to climate change among fishers and farmers in the Pacific region (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75610).

livestock (general/small ruminants).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized livestock adaptation and mitigation strategies across various regions. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed heat stress impacts on cattle production and adaptation measures (Source 1).
- CGIAR evaluated extreme heat stress risks and adaptation challenges for livestock (Source 2).
- CGIAR promoted climate-smart agriculture practices for resilient livelihoods (Source 3).
- CGIAR quantified GHG emissions mitigation opportunities in crop and livestock production (Source 4).
- CGIAR analyzed the mitigation potential of compost in livestock systems (Source 5).
- CGIAR studied the effectiveness of REDD+ projects in reducing deforestation linked to cattle ranching (Source 6).
- CGIAR examined climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers (Source 7).
- CGIAR investigated GHG emissions reduction through intensified pasture management (Source 8).
livestock (general/small ruminants).adaptation:
• CGIAR estimated heat stress-induced losses in cattle production and proposed adaptation strategies like switching to heat-tolerant breeds and providing shade, ventilation, and cooling systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/s2542-5196(22)00002-x).
• CGIAR assessed the increasing risk of extreme heat stress for livestock species and the challenges of adaptation in outdoor livestock keeping (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.15825).
• CGIAR promoted climate-smart agriculture practices, including improved crop varieties and agroforestry, to enhance climate resilience among smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.3390/cli5030063).
• CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate variability on grazing herds and identified adaptation responses available to farmers in semi-arid rangelands (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001).
• CGIAR examined climate change adaptation strategies among smallholder farmers in Senegal’s semi-arid zone, highlighting the role of socio-economic and institutional supports (https://doi.org/10.3389/fclim.2024.1332196).
livestock (general/small ruminants).mitigation:
• CGIAR quantified opportunities for greenhouse gas emissions mitigation in crop and livestock production, identifying cost-effective options for smallholder farmers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.147344).
• CGIAR developed the LCA4CSA framework to assess the mitigation potential of compost and other climate-smart agriculture practices in livestock systems (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2019.02.001).
• CGIAR evaluated the effectiveness of a REDD+ project in reducing deforestation and emissions from cattle ranching in the Brazilian Amazon (https://doi.org/10.1093/ajae/aay028).
• CGIAR investigated the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions through intensified pasture management in cattle farms in the Brazilian Amazon (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2017.06.130).
livestock (general/small ruminants).water: • CGIAR analyzed the impacts of climate change, including a 30% reduction in precipitation, on land use and livestock production in the Brazilian Amazon (https://doi.org/10.1175/2010ei333.1).

dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient dryland cereals (sorghum/millets) through genetic modification, adaptation strategies, and climate risk assessments. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed drought and heat-tolerant pearl millet cultivars (Source 1). 
- CGIAR assessed climate change impacts on sorghum production in India (Source 2).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed yield gains under climate change due to genetic modification of pearl millet, enhancing drought and heat tolerance (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.06.002).
• CGIAR analyzed vulnerability of sorghum to climate change in India, proposing low-cost adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012).
• CGIAR combined genomic and climatic characterization of Ethiopian barley to guide breeding efforts for climate adaptation (https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.16560).
• CGIAR used the APSIM model to test management practices and improved varieties for enhancing sorghum adaptive capacity under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685).
• CGIAR explored adaptation strategies for pearl millet production under climate change in West Africa, focusing on cultivar type and planting windows (https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630).
• CGIAR increased access to sorghum genetic resources adapted to agro-climatic conditions in Burkina Faso (https://doi.org/10.1017/s0014479713000616).
• CGIAR conducted a climate risk assessment for sorghum in Tanzania, highlighting stagnated yields despite climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719).
• CGIAR described the status and utility of pearl millet germplasm for crop improvement, identifying trait-specific sources for abiotic stress resistance (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-99-5890-0_2).
• CGIAR conducted a climate risk assessment for sorghum in Kenya, noting its production in areas with low agricultural potential (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107722).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).water: • CGIAR evaluated hydro-priming with different water sources to improve sorghum seed germination and seedling development under early-season drought conditions (https://doi.org/10.3390/atmos12040419).

livestock (dairy).narrative: CGIAR prioritized livestock (dairy) research through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Specifically:
livestock (dairy).adaptation:
• CGIAR developed fodder tree practices to improve livestock productivity and smallholder livelihoods in Africa, particularly in the highlands of Eastern Africa, where over 200,000 smallholders plant them to feed dairy cows, meeting production shortages during extreme climatic conditions such as droughts (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cosust.2013.11.008).
• CGIAR investigated projected climate change impacts on forage grasses for Ethiopian dairy systems, determining geographical suitability and ability to meet predicted dry matter demand under various scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w).
• CGIAR analyzed gendered trade-offs for women milk marketers in Kenya, highlighting the contested nature of gender market relations under agricultural intensification and commercialization pursued under low emissions dairy development (LEDD) (https://doi.org/10.1111/ruso.12345).
livestock (dairy).mitigation:
• CGIAR assessed the contribution of sectoral climate change mitigation options to national targets, focusing on feed and manure management scenarios in Kenya's dairy production region (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/aaac84).
• CGIAR evaluated the greenhouse gas mitigation potential of improved feeding practices and increased yields of feed crops in Tanzania’s southern highlands region (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-83475-8).
• CGIAR analyzed the drivers of forest disturbance and their relationship with dairy intensification in Kenyan montane forests, showing that intensification leads to both farm efficiency gains and reduced forest disturbance (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2018.06.011).
• CGIAR evaluated dairy cattle models for predicting milk production and their adequacy in determining ruminants’ worldwide contribution to greenhouse gas emissions (https://doi.org/10.1071/an14620).
• CGIAR explored improving emission quantification methods for methane emissions from manure management in the Canadian dairy industry (https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/8/3/035008).
• CGIAR assessed the potential for commercial hay production in Kenya to increase fodder supply, reduce GHG emission intensity, and support financially viable business models (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93176).
• CGIAR investigated changes in food loss and waste in dairy supply chains, showing that interventions could provide meaningful greenhouse mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/89103).

bananas.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized banana research through climate-focused innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR assessed climate suitability for banana production (Source 1).
- CGIAR modeled disease risk under climate change (Source 2).
- CGIAR developed drought-tolerant banana cultivars (Source 3).
bananas.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed BXW disease risk under current and future climates to guide early warning and control (https://doi.org/10.1094/pdis-07-22-1672-re).
• CGIAR analyzed transcriptomic responses of banana corms to Fusarium oxysporum infection (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG).
• CGIAR evaluated banana genotypes under osmotic stress conditions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q0ESWS).
• CGIAR empowered the export banana sector in climate-smart decision-making (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121261).
bananas.water:
• CGIAR assessed climate suitability and disease pressure for banana production, identifying strategic irrigation opportunities (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0220601).
• CGIAR investigated drought tolerance in banana cultivars to reduce dependency on irrigation water (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68445).
• CGIAR used growth and transpiration phenotyping to select water-efficient banana genotypes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5).

livestock (small).narrative: CGIAR prioritized small livestock research across multiple themes, including climate adaptation, mitigation, and water resource management. Specifically:
livestock (small).adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed the impact of climate change on the productivity and adaptation of Ethiopia's Bonga and Menz sheep breeds, deriving novel phenotypes of sheep resilience and response to climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.livsci.2024.105633).
• CGIAR conducted sheep feeding trials in Mali to promote sustainable intensification of mixed crop-livestock systems for better food security and improved livelihoods (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BUMYV0).
• CGIAR explored homemade concentrate preparation for fattening sheep to support climate change adaptation in livestock feeding practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168846).
livestock (small).mitigation: • CGIAR compared Tier 1 and 2 methodologies for estimating intake and enteric methane emission factors from smallholder sheep systems in Ethiopia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6).
livestock (small).water: • CGIAR evaluated the use of irrigated oat-vetch fodder for sheep fattening during the dry period in Ethiopia, utilizing water from shallow wells and manual pumps (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DQGTMP).

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient fruit and vegetable production through targeted research. Specifically: 
- CGIAR assessed climate suitability for macadamia in Malawi (Source 1). 
- CGIAR mapped mango suitability in Cambodia (Source 3).
fruits and vegetables.adaptation:
• CGIAR examined climate change impacts on macadamia suitability in Malawi, emphasizing area-specific adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0257007).
• CGIAR evaluated future mango suitability in Nepal, highlighting implications for fruit zoning and climate adaptation planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178854).
• CGIAR conducted a systematic review of climate change adaptation in vegetable farming systems in Africa, focusing on water-related hazards and technological responses (https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7).
• CGIAR documented crop diversification and small pump installation for vegetable production in Burkina Faso (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
fruits and vegetables.water:
• CGIAR surveyed vegetable value chain productivity and adoption of modern inputs in Ethiopia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q55PL6).
• CGIAR mapped mango suitability in the Mekong Delta, Cambodia, considering irrigation and other technological interventions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173189).
• CGIAR reviewed climate change adaptation in vegetable farming systems in Africa, addressing water-related hazards like drought and flooding (https://doi.org/10.1038/s43247-025-02763-7).
• CGIAR documented the use of a small pump for vegetable production in Burkina Faso (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).

forages and pastures.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized forages and pastures research through adaptation-focused innovations. Specifically: 
- CGIAR developed methodologies for agronomic evaluation of tropical pastures to assess germplasm adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS). 
- The Center evaluated the adaptation potential of pasture germplasm under different grazing management systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
forages and pastures.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed a manual for agronomic evaluation of tropical pastures to assess germplasm adaptation across distinct ecosystems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DYR7KS).
• CGIAR evaluated the adaptation potential of pasture germplasm under different grazing management systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
• CGIAR selected Brachiaria accessions with superior agronomic traits and assessed the effect of climate and soils on yield (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).
• CGIAR studied genotype x environment interactions in Desmodium ovalifolium to identify soil fertility and climate effects on tannin contents (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).
• CGIAR assessed germplasm adaptation to the Quilichao environment, including yield potential and performance during severe dry seasons (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VGOIIQ).
• CGIAR evaluated land suitability and potential climate change impacts for alfalfa production in Ethiopia (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).
• CGIAR facilitated knowledge transfer and international collaboration for pasture and agronomy systems research through the CLIFF-GRADS program (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110096).
forages and pastures.water: • CGIAR evaluated land suitability and supplemental irrigation needs for alfalfa production in Ethiopia, considering climate change impacts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).

general cereals.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized climate-resilient cereal production in India through adaptive strategies and water management innovations. Specifically:
- CGIAR evaluated the long-term and short-term effects of climatic and non-climatic variables on cereal production (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403).
- CGIAR re-designed irrigated intensive cereal systems through precision agronomic innovations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
general cereals.adaptation: • CGIAR evaluated the long-term and short-term effects of climatic and non-climatic variables on cereal production, emphasizing the need for proactive measures to reduce adverse impacts of climate change (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.heliyon.2024.e36403).
general cereals.water: • CGIAR re-designed irrigated intensive cereal systems through bundling precision agronomic innovations for agricultural sustainability in North-West India (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
